kareri-village-trek.title: Ghera to Kareri Village and Back
kareri-village-trek.trailType: Riverside forest trail with rocky sections along the Nyund Khad
kareri-village-trek.accommodation: Day hike; return to a Dharamshala guesthouse
kareri-village-trek.description: The trek leaves the roadhead at Ghera and immediately drops to cross the Nyund Khad on a wooden footbridge, the stream running fast and cold even in summer from Dhauladhar snowmelt. From the crossing, a well-trodden mule path climbs through terraced fields of maize and wheat worked by Gaddi families, their slate-roofed houses stacked along the hillside in loose clusters. Deodar and horse-chestnut give way to open scrub as the gradient steepens past the halfway mark, and the first proper views of the Dhauladhar wall open up to the north, a serrated grey rampart that seems to lean over the valley. Kareri village itself sits on a shelf above the ravine, its houses built from mortarless stone with slate slabs weighing down wooden roofs, a construction style unchanged for generations. Walk through the lanes to the small Shiva temple at the village edge, where locals often gather in the afternoon, and refill water bottles at the spring below the last house before turning back. The return leg retraces the same path but feels different in the changing light, with the ridge above Triund catching the last sun while the valley below slips into shadow. Afternoons here can turn windy without warning, so plan to be off the exposed upper sections before 3pm. Carry enough water for the full day since reliable sources thin out above the second stream crossing, and wear ankle-supporting shoes for the loose scree near the village approach. Gaddi shepherds graze flocks on these slopes right through early autumn, and their dogs, while generally indifferent to trekkers, are best given a wide berth. This is one of the few Dharamshala day hikes that delivers genuine village culture alongside mountain scenery without needing a tent.
highlights: Traditional Gaddi slate-roof architecture in Kareri village
Close-up views of the Dhauladhar rock wall
Terraced farmland and mule trails
Nyund Khad stream crossings
Village Shiva temple
tips: Start by 8am to avoid afternoon wind on exposed sections
Carry all drinking water; sources are limited above the second crossing
Wear shoes with good ankle support for loose scree near the village

guna-devi-trek.title: Dharamkot to Guna Devi Temple and Back
guna-devi-trek.trailType: Shaded forest trail through oak and deodar with a rock-cut final approach
guna-devi-trek.accommodation: Day hike; return to McLeod Ganj/Dharamkot guesthouse
guna-devi-trek.description: From Dharamkot's upper lane, the trail slips into a stand of oak and rhododendron almost immediately, the canopy thick enough to keep the path cool even at midday. Langurs are a near-constant presence in the branches overhead, unbothered by passing trekkers, and woodpeckers can often be heard drumming somewhere out of sight. The route contours along the hillside rather than climbing directly, crossing two seasonal streams where the rocks are often slick, before the forest thins and the cliff housing Guna Devi comes into view. The temple itself is a modest shrine built into a natural rock alcove, with a perennial spring trickling from the cliff face just beside it, a good spot to top up water and rest in the shade. Local families sometimes make the walk on auspicious days to leave offerings, and the atmosphere at the shrine is quiet rather than touristy. Beyond the temple, a short scramble up loose rock leads to a small ledge with a clear view down toward the Kangra valley and, on clear mornings, a hazy outline of the plains far below. The descent follows the same forest trail, though the light filtering through the canopy changes considerably by afternoon, and the stream crossings that felt straightforward on the way up can feel more technical when tired legs are involved. This walk works well as a half-day acclimatization outing for trekkers based in McLeod Ganj who want forest cover and a cultural stop rather than open ridge walking. Afternoon mist can roll in through the trees by 3-4pm in monsoon months, reducing visibility on the upper section, so plan the return accordingly. Leeches are a genuine nuisance during the monsoon, and gaiters or salt are worth carrying.
highlights: Cave shrine of Guna Devi built into a cliff face
Dense oak and rhododendron forest with resident langurs
Perennial spring beside the temple
Distant views toward the Kangra valley
Quiet pilgrimage atmosphere away from McLeod Ganj crowds
tips: Carry salt or wear gaiters during monsoon due to leeches
Start early to avoid afternoon mist in the forest canopy
Refill water at the spring beside the temple

bhagsu-waterfall-trek.title: Bhagsunag Temple to Bhagsu Waterfall
bhagsu-waterfall-trek.trailType: Paved lanes giving way to a rocky streamside path
bhagsu-waterfall-trek.accommodation: Day hike; return to McLeod Ganj
bhagsu-waterfall-trek.description: The walk begins at the Bhagsunag temple, a functioning Shiva shrine with a small tank of icy spring water where pilgrims bathe before continuing uphill. From here the lane narrows into a stone path lined with cafes serving Israeli and Tibetan food, a reminder that this is one of the most visited short walks in the entire Dharamshala area. Past the last cluster of shops, the trail turns rougher, following the stream bed over rounded boulders that are slippery when wet, up to the lower tier of the Bhagsu waterfall where most day visitors stop to wade in the pool at its base. A steeper, less-used path continues past this point to a smaller upper cascade, quieter and considerably less crowded, reached by a short scramble over wet rock that demands proper footwear rather than sandals. The upper pool sits in a rocky amphitheater with spray drifting across it most of the day, and it is a good spot to pause before the volume of visitors picks up again lower down. Water levels and force vary enormously by season, post-monsoon the falls run full and forceful, while by late winter they can thin to a trickle. Swimming below the falls is popular but the rocks stay slick year-round, and slips are the most common mishap on this otherwise gentle outing. The return route is identical, downhill nearly the whole way, and the walk can comfortably be paired with a visit to Bhagsunag temple or a longer loop toward Dharamkot for those wanting more distance. Best visited early morning or late afternoon to avoid the midday crowds that build steadily through the day.
highlights: Bhagsunag Shiva temple with a spring-fed bathing tank
Two-tier waterfall with a swimming pool at the base
Quieter upper cascade reached by a short scramble
Cafes and Tibetan/Israeli food stalls along the trail
Easy access from McLeod Ganj
tips: Wear grippy shoes; the rocks near the falls stay slippery
Visit early morning or late afternoon to avoid crowds
Carry a change of clothes if you plan to wade in the pool

dharamkot-trek.title: McLeod Ganj to Dharamkot and Gallu Devi Loop
dharamkot-trek.accommodation: Day hike; return to McLeod Ganj
dharamkot-trek.description: Leaving McLeod Ganj's main square behind, the lane to Dharamkot climbs gently past guesthouses, yoga studios and small organic cafes that have made this hillside village something of a wellness retreat in its own right over the past two decades. Beyond the last row of buildings, the path turns to packed earth and enters a belt of deodar forest, opening periodically onto grassy clearings where cows graze and paragliders sometimes launch on calm mornings. A left fork leads up through progressively thinning trees to Gallu Devi temple, a small hilltop shrine that also happens to be one of the better viewpoints in the immediate Dharamshala area, with the Kangra valley spread out to the south and the Dhauladhar ridge rearing up close behind to the north. From the temple, an optional side trail continues further uphill toward Lahesh Cave and Triund for those wanting extra distance, though the standard loop turns back through a different section of forest, dropping past a scatter of homestays and small farms back toward Dharamkot village. The return into McLeod Ganj can be varied by taking the road through Bhagsu instead of retracing the direct path, adding waterfall views to the day without much extra walking. Weather on this route is generally forgiving compared to the higher trails, though afternoon cloud can build quickly in monsoon season and obscure the valley views that make the walk worthwhile. The gentle gradient and short distance make this a sensible first outing for trekkers newly arrived at altitude, easing the body into the terrain before attempting Triund or anything further into the Dhauladhar.
highlights: Gallu Devi temple viewpoint over the Kangra valley
Dharamkot's cafe and yoga culture
Paragliding launch point on clear mornings
Optional extension toward Lahesh Cave
Gentle acclimatization walk from McLeod Ganj
tips: Combine with a return via Bhagsu for waterfall views
Good first-day acclimatization walk before attempting Triund
Watch for afternoon cloud buildup in monsoon months

naddi-trek.title: Naddi Village Orchard and Ridge Viewpoint Walk
naddi-trek.trailType: Village road and grassy ridge path
naddi-trek.accommodation: Day hike; return to Dharamshala/McLeod Ganj
naddi-trek.description: Naddi's claim to fame among Dharamshala regulars is the clean, unobstructed view it offers of the entire Dhauladhar face, and this walk is built around reaching the best vantage points for that view. From the village square, a quiet lane climbs past apple and pear orchards fenced with loose stone walls, the kind of small-holding agriculture that still supports many local families here. The paragliding takeoff ground appears after twenty minutes or so, a grassy shelf where pilots wait for the right thermals before launching toward Dharamshala below, and it makes a natural first stop for photographs. Continuing past the takeoff point, a narrower dirt track climbs onto the ridge above the village, gaining just enough height to reveal the full sweep of the range from Moon Peak in the west to the peaks above Kareri in the east, with almost no tree cover to interrupt the panorama. This upper ridge catches wind steadily through the afternoon, which is exactly why paragliders favor it, so a light windproof layer is worth having even on an otherwise warm day. The walk back down passes a cluster of tea stalls that have sprung up to serve the steady trickle of day visitors, a good place to sit before returning to the village. Because the trail stays low and the distance is short, this is an ideal outing for travelers who want serious mountain views without serious effort, or for those still adjusting to the altitude after arriving in Dharamshala. Morning visits generally offer the clearest views, since haze tends to build over the valley by early afternoon.
highlights: Uninterrupted panoramic view of the Dhauladhar range
Paragliding takeoff ground
Apple and pear orchard walks
Low-effort, high-reward viewpoint trail
Tea stalls with valley views
tips: Visit in the morning for the clearest views before valley haze builds up
Carry a light windproof layer for the exposed ridge
Combine with the nearby Dal Lake walk for a fuller day

dal-lake-trail.title: Naddi to Dal Lake Forest Walk
dal-lake-trail.trailType: Forest path through deodar to a lakeside shrine
dal-lake-trail.description: Despite sharing its name with Kashmir's famous lake, this Dal Lake is a modest, almost secretive body of water tucked into a hollow of deodar forest below Naddi, and the walk to reach it is as much about the forest as the destination. The path leaves the Naddi-Dharamshala road and drops gently through tall deodar trees whose canopy blocks out most direct sunlight, keeping the trail cool and slightly damp underfoot even in summer. Prayer flags start appearing strung between trunks as the lake nears, marking the approach to a small temple dedicated to the sage Durvasa Rishi that sits right at the water's edge. The lake itself is often carpeted with lily pads and hyacinth, its surface still enough to mirror the surrounding trees, and resident fish can sometimes be seen near the shallow edges where visitors toss bits of food. A short circuit around the water passes a scatter of benches and a handful of tea stalls, busiest during the annual Dal Lake fair held here in September, when the whole community gathers for a mela with music and local crafts. Outside of that one week, the spot tends to be genuinely quiet, visited mostly by locals out for a walk rather than trekking groups. From the far side of the lake, a connecting trail climbs back up toward Naddi village, offering the option to combine both walks into a single half-day outing. The forest floor gets slippery after rain, so trekking poles or careful footing help on the descent, and mosquitoes can be persistent near the water in warmer months.
highlights: Small forest-ringed lake distinct from Kashmir's Dal Lake
Durvasa Rishi temple at the water's edge
Deodar forest walk
Annual September lake fair
Easy combination with the Naddi viewpoint walk
tips: Visit outside the September mela week for a quieter experience
Carry mosquito repellent in warmer months
Watch your footing on the descent after rain

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund: Expedition Start and Load Check
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: The expedition begins at the forest checkpost above McLeod Ganj, where permits are logged and porters distribute loads for the ten-day carry to come. Unlike a standard Triund day trek, this stage doubles as a shakedown day, boots, packs and rhythm all get tested on the same familiar ground that eventually leads much higher. The trail switchbacks steadily through mixed oak and rhododendron forest, gaining height in a series of long traverses rather than a single hard pull, and Gallu Devi temple offers a natural break point roughly a third of the way up. Above the treeline the vegetation drops away to scrub and open grass, and the first real sense of scale arrives, the Dhauladhar wall stops being a distant backdrop and becomes the thing you're actually walking toward. Triund's broad grassy shoulder, dotted with tea-hut camps and grazing mules, serves as the expedition's first proper camp, chosen deliberately as a low-altitude buffer before the harder ground above. Team gear gets sorted and re-weighed here, and the guide runs through the plan for the technical sections still days away: rope work, glacier travel, summit weather windows. Sunset from Triund, with the Kangra valley falling into haze below and the Dhauladhar catching the day's last light, is as much a psychological milestone as a scenic one, marking the actual start of committing to the mountain. Weather at this stage is usually mild, but afternoon cloud build-up is common and occasional light rain isn't unusual even outside monsoon, so tents are pitched with drainage in mind. Water is plentiful from the stream just below camp. Rest is prioritized this first night since the following days offer no easy recovery ground, and expedition members are encouraged to sleep as much as possible while altitude gain is still gentle.
highlights: Shakedown day on familiar Triund trail before harder terrain
Gallu Devi temple rest point
First full view of the Dhauladhar wall as a climbing objective
Gear sorting and expedition briefing at Triund camp
tips: Sleep as much as possible while altitude gain is still gentle
Pitch tents with drainage in mind; afternoon rain is common
Carry enough water from the stream below camp

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Triund to Lahesh Cave: First High Camp
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.accommodation: Cave shelter / tented camp at Lahesh Cave
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: Leaving Triund's grassy shoulder, the route immediately turns rockier, following a narrow ridge trail that climbs through the last stunted birch before the landscape turns fully alpine. This is the stage where the expedition's pace typically slows to something more deliberate, loads adjusted for the terrain and the team spacing out along the ridge rather than moving as a tight group. Lahesh Cave itself is a broad overhang of grey rock, large enough to shelter tents and gear from wind, and has served climbing parties heading toward Indrahar and beyond for decades. Camp here sits just above 11,000 feet, cold enough at night that down jackets come out for the first time, though days remain generally pleasant if the sky stays clear. From the cave, the views open dramatically, Triund and the Kangra valley recede below, while ahead the jagged skyline of peaks toward Indrahar Pass and the Hanuman Tibba massif starts to reveal itself properly for the first time. The rock face nearby holds a small seasonal trickle of meltwater in season, a useful backup source though not always reliable, so carrying enough from lower streams is prudent. This is also typically the last night with any real vegetation nearby, and the guide usually uses the evening to brief the team on glacier travel protocols for the days ahead: rope-up procedures, crevasse awareness, and pacing for the altitude gain still to come. Wind tends to funnel through the cave mouth after dark, so tent placement matters more here than it did at Triund. Expedition members should expect noticeably colder nights from this point forward and adjust sleeping systems accordingly.
highlights: Lahesh Cave rock shelter camp above 11,000 ft
First clear views toward Indrahar Pass and the Hanuman Tibba massif
Rope and crevasse-awareness briefing
Transition from forest to full alpine terrain
tips: Bring out down jackets; nights turn genuinely cold from here
Don't rely solely on the seasonal meltwater trickle near camp
Choose tent spots away from the wind-funneling cave mouth

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Lahesh Cave to Chata Parao: Indrahar Base Camp
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: Above Lahesh Cave, the trail abandons anything resembling a maintained path and becomes a sustained scramble over broken rock and old moraine debris left by retreating ice. Cairns mark the general line, but route-finding becomes a genuine skill here rather than a formality, and the pace of the group slows considerably compared to the previous two days. The terrain steepens in pulses, alternating between loose scree that shifts underfoot and firmer boulder sections that demand careful hand placement. Chata Parao, the traditional base camp for parties attempting Indrahar Pass and the approach toward Hanuman Tibba, sits on a relatively flat bench of ground below the main headwall, ringed by peaks that block the wind from most directions, a genuine luxury at this altitude. Reaching camp usually takes most of the day even though the distance is short, since the terrain rarely allows fast movement. This is the point where the expedition transitions from trekking gear to mountaineering mindset: harnesses, crampons and ice axes get inspected and distributed here for the first time, and anyone with gear issues needs to flag them now rather than higher up. Water comes from a glacial stream just below camp, milky with rock flour and best filtered or settled before drinking. Nights at Chata Parao regularly drop well below freezing, and the psychological shift from trekking to climbing tends to set in strongly here, as the true scale of the peaks above becomes unavoidable. Cloud often builds over the ridgeline by mid-afternoon, occasionally bringing brief snow flurries even in the pre-monsoon season, so the team times the final approach to arrive before early afternoon.
highlights: Chata Parao base camp below the Indrahar headwall
First distribution of mountaineering gear: harness, crampons, ice axe
Route-finding over moraine and broken rock
Wind-sheltered camp ringed by peaks
tips: Filter or settle glacial meltwater before drinking
Flag any gear issues now, before terrain gets harder
Aim to reach camp before early-afternoon cloud builds

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Acclimatization Day: Minkiani Ridge Climb
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.accommodation: Tented camp at Chata Parao (second night)
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: With three days of steady altitude gain behind the team, a dedicated rest-and-acclimatize day is built into the schedule at Chata Parao, following the standard mountaineering principle of climbing high and sleeping low. Rather than sitting idle, the morning is used for a supervised acclimatization hike up toward the Minkiani ridge, gaining roughly nine hundred feet before returning to sleep at the lower camp altitude. The climb up loose scree and broken rock is short but genuinely tiring at this elevation, with breathing noticeably harder than the same effort would require lower down, a useful, if humbling, reminder of how much the body still needs to adjust. From the high point on the ridge, the view extends across a jumble of unnamed minor summits toward the main Hanuman Tibba massif, giving the team its first real look at the upper mountain and the glacier approach still ahead. The guide uses this vantage point to talk through the planned route, pointing out the advance base camp site and the general line toward high camp. Descending back to Chata Parao by early afternoon leaves time for equipment checks, rope practice on a safe patch of ground, and a longer rest before the harder carries begin the next day. Symptoms of altitude, headache, mild nausea, disturbed sleep, are actively monitored during this day, and anyone showing signs of trouble has the option to descend rather than push on, a decision the expedition takes seriously given how remote the terrain becomes above this point. Afternoon weather again tends toward cloud and occasional light snow, reinforcing the importance of finishing the acclimatization hike before midday.
highlights: Acclimatization climb to Minkiani ridge
First close view of the Hanuman Tibba massif and glacier line
Rope practice on safe ground
Guide's route briefing from the ridge vantage point
tips: Report any altitude symptoms honestly; descent is always an option
Finish the acclimatization hike before midday cloud arrives
Use the afternoon for rest, not extra exertion

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Chata Parao to Advance Base Camp
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.trailType: Glacial moraine and boulder-hopping along the glacier margin
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: The route out of Chata Parao climbs directly onto the lateral moraine of the glacier feeding the northern flank of Hanuman Tibba, and from here the character of the trek changes entirely, this is glacier travel, not trekking, even though ropes may not yet be strictly necessary on this stretch. Boulder-hopping across unstable moraine demands constant attention, ankles and knees absorbing the uneven impact of rock that shifts without warning underfoot. Meltwater channels cut across the route at irregular intervals, some easily stepped over and others requiring a short detour to find a safe crossing point. As height is gained, the moraine gradually gives way to cleaner ice and firmer footing near the glacier's edge, and the advance base camp site appears as a rare patch of stable ground tucked against a rock rib that offers some shelter from wind funneling down off the summit ridge. From ABC, Hanuman Tibba's upper pyramid becomes fully visible for the first time, close enough now that individual rock bands and snow gullies on the final approach can be studied and discussed. This camp marks the true committal point of the expedition, everything above here is technical climbing rather than trekking, and the guide spends the evening running through the summit day plan in detail, including turnaround times and contingency options if weather deteriorates. Temperatures drop sharply after sunset, and water sourced from glacial melt needs extra time to settle before filtering. The physical toll of the day's boulder-hopping is considerable, and an early camp routine is enforced to maximize rest before the technical days ahead, when sleep becomes a genuinely scarce resource.
highlights: Committal crossing of the glacier's lateral moraine
First full view of Hanuman Tibba's summit pyramid
Advance base camp tucked against a wind-sheltering rock rib
Summit-day planning and turnaround-time briefing
tips: Move deliberately on moraine; footing shifts without warning
Allow extra time for glacial meltwater to settle before filtering
Prioritize rest immediately; sleep becomes scarce from here

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Advance Base Camp to High Camp
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.trailType: Snow and scree slopes with a fixed-rope section
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: Above advance base camp, the route steepens onto consolidated snow and ice, and the team ropes up for the first sustained roped travel of the expedition, moving in a staggered line with fixed spacing to manage crevasse risk on the upper glacier. Crampons go on almost immediately, and the rhythm of the day slows to the careful, deliberate pace that high-altitude snow travel demands: short steps, controlled breathing, regular checks on rope tension between climbers. A short section of fixed rope, placed earlier in the season by the guiding team, helps negotiate a steeper ice slope roughly two-thirds of the way to high camp, and this is where technical competence genuinely matters rather than just fitness. High camp itself is a small, exposed platform cut into the snow, large enough for two or three tents and anchored carefully against the wind that rarely lets up completely at this altitude. The air feels noticeably thin here, conversations shorten, and even routine camp chores like melting snow for water take considerably longer than they would lower down. From this camp, the final summit approach is visible in full, a mix of snow slopes and rock bands leading to the top, and the guide finalizes the exact timing for the summit push based on the evening's weather observations. Sleep at high camp is typically poor regardless of tiredness, a near-universal experience at this elevation, and the team is encouraged to rest rather than expect deep sleep. Gear is laid out and checked meticulously before dark, since a pre-dawn departure leaves little room for fumbling with headlamps or frozen buckles in the cold.
highlights: First sustained roped glacier travel of the expedition
Fixed-rope section on a steep ice slope
Small, wind-anchored high camp platform
Full view of the final summit approach
tips: Move in short, controlled steps; the air is noticeably thinner here
Lay out gear before dark to avoid fumbling in the cold
Expect poor sleep regardless of fatigue; rest anyway

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Summit Push: Hanuman Ka Tibba and Return to ABC
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.trailType: Glacier, snow and ice with fixed-rope sections
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: The alarm goes off well before first light, and the team departs high camp in darkness, headlamps picking out the fixed line and wands marking the route across the upper snowfield. The initial hour is the coldest of the entire expedition, temperatures well below freezing and wind chill making exposed skin a real concern, but movement gradually warms the body as the gradient steepens toward the first rock band. Here the route alternates between snow gullies and short rock steps, the climbing straightforward but unforgiving of mistakes at this altitude, and the rope team moves methodically rather than quickly. As dawn breaks, the surrounding Dhauladhar and distant Pir Panjal ranges emerge in layers of pink and grey, a reward that most climbers say makes the pre-dawn cold worthwhile. The final approach to the summit ridge narrows considerably, exposed on both sides, and the last section demands full concentration and, conditions permitting, careful use of the fixed rope for security. Reaching the top of Hanuman Tibba delivers a view that few Dhauladhar treks can match, the entire western Himalayan crest visible in a single sweep, from the Kullu peaks to the plains haze far to the south. Time on the summit is kept deliberately short given the altitude and the long descent still ahead. The way down retraces the ascent line, generally faster but with legs and concentration both fading, and the team pushes all the way back to advance base camp by evening, a long and demanding descent day that tests reserves built up over the preceding week. Weather windows for this day are chosen conservatively, and turning back before the summit remains an accepted, sometimes necessary, outcome.
highlights: Pre-dawn headlamp ascent across the upper snowfield
Sunrise over the Dhauladhar and distant Pir Panjal
Summit of Hanuman Tibba with a full western Himalayan panorama
Long roped descent back to advance base camp
tips: Dress for the coldest hour of the trip in the pre-dawn start
Keep summit time short given the altitude
Respect conservative turnaround times, even close to the top

hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.title: Advance Base Camp to McLeod Ganj: Long Descent
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.trailType: Moraine, ridge and forest trail descending through multiple ecological zones
hanuman-ka-tibba-expedition.description: With the summit attempt behind them, the team breaks camp at advance base camp for the last time and begins the long retreat down through terrain that feels considerably easier now that the technical climbing is finished, though the sheer distance makes this one of the longest single days of the entire expedition. The moraine crossing that felt so demanding on the way up now passes more quickly with the benefit of a known route, though tired knees make the boulder-hopping no less jarring. Chata Parao appears almost unrecognizable in the changed light of descent, and the team pauses only briefly before continuing down past Lahesh Cave, the familiar overhang now marking roughly the halfway point of the day's descent. Below the cave, the transition back into forest happens quickly, birch, then rhododendron, then full oak canopy, and with it comes a noticeable easing of both the cold and the thin air that has defined the past week. Triund arrives in mid-afternoon for most groups, offering a final chance to rest and eat before the last stretch down to McLeod Ganj, though strong parties sometimes push through without a long stop. The final descent through switchbacked forest trail feels almost gentle after the glacier and rock of the past days, though accumulated fatigue makes even easy ground demanding by the end. Arriving back in McLeod Ganj's bustling lanes after more than a week on the mountain is its own kind of disorientation, the noise and warmth of the town a stark contrast to the silence of high camp. A hot shower, a real bed, and a debrief dinner typically close out the expedition, with porters and guides celebrating a summit push that few trekkers in the region ever attempt.
highlights: Long single-day descent from glacier to forest
Familiar landmarks (Chata Parao, Lahesh Cave, Triund) in reverse
Rapid transition through alpine, scrub and forest zones
Return to McLeod Ganj after more than a week on the mountain
tips: Use trekking poles; knees take a beating on the moraine descent
Eat and rest at Triund before the final push if tired
Plan a rest day in McLeod Ganj after the expedition ends

moon-peak-trek.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund
moon-peak-trek.description: The trail to Triund leaves McLeod Ganj through a final stretch of market lanes before diving into forest just past the small forest checkpost, where entry is logged for anyone heading further into the Dhauladhar. Deodar gives way to oak, and periodic clearings offer the first proper views back over the Kangra valley, hazy and sprawling in the mid-morning light. Mule trains pass regularly on this stretch, ferrying supplies up to the tea-hut camps at Triund, and their bells are often audible well before the animals themselves come into view around a bend. The gradient builds steadily rather than sharply, and most groups find a natural rhythm of walk-and-rest blocks that gets them to the ridge by early afternoon. Triund itself opens up suddenly, a wide, grassy shoulder with the Dhauladhar's grey rock face rising almost vertically just beyond, close enough that the scale can feel slightly disorienting after a morning spent looking at it from a distance. Tea huts here serve Maggi noodles and sweet chai to a mix of day-trippers and overnight trekkers, and the atmosphere in the evening tends toward genuinely festive, with groups clustered around whichever hut has the best view for sunset. Camp is pitched on the grass a little apart from the huts for a quieter night, and temperatures drop noticeably once the sun disappears behind the ridge. This first night at altitude is generally comfortable, an easy introduction before the terrain and air both grow considerably more serious over the following two days toward Moon Peak.
highlights: Forest ascent from McLeod Ganj through oak and deodar
Mule supply trains on the Triund trail
Sudden, close-up reveal of the Dhauladhar wall at Triund
Sunset over the Kangra valley from the ridge
tips: Register at the forest checkpost before starting
Pace yourself with regular short breaks rather than one long push
Camp slightly away from the tea huts for a quieter night

moon-peak-trek.title: Triund to Lahesh Cave via the Snowline
moon-peak-trek.trailType: Rocky ridge trail past the Snowline section
moon-peak-trek.description: Above Triund the trail narrows and the grass gives way to a rockier, more broken surface as the path climbs onto the ridge proper, with the last isolated birch trees marking the practical end of the treeline. This stretch is often called the Snowline section locally, since patches of old snow can linger here well into early summer in shaded folds of the rock even when the rest of the trail is clear. Moon Peak itself becomes visible for the first time from a bend partway up, a rounded rocky summit standing slightly apart from the jagged main Dhauladhar skyline, distinctive enough that guides use it as a landmark for gauging progress. Lahesh Cave, the day's camp, is a wide rock overhang that has sheltered generations of shepherds and trekkers alike, deep enough to pitch tents fully out of the wind and rain. The short remaining daylight after arrival is usually spent exploring the immediate area, a low ridge just above the cave gives an even better look at tomorrow's route toward Moon Peak, letting the group visualize the climb ahead over dinner. Nights here are considerably colder than Triund, and condensation inside the cave overhang can be an issue for anyone not using a proper sleeping system. Water is sourced from a small stream a short walk below camp, running clear but cold enough to numb fingers quickly. This stage marks the real shift in character of the trek, from a straightforward forest walk to genuine high-altitude terrain, and most groups treat the evening as a final rest before the demanding push to Moon Peak the next day.
highlights: Snowline section with lingering snow patches
First clear sighting of Moon Peak's rounded summit
Overnight shelter in the historic Lahesh Cave overhang
Ridge viewpoint above camp for route visualization
tips: Use a cave spot for shelter but check for condensation and drips
Fill water bottles at the stream below camp before dark
Layer up; nights here are noticeably colder than Triund

moon-peak-trek.title: Moon Peak Ridge and Back to Lahesh Cave
moon-peak-trek.trailType: Steep scree and boulder ridge with occasional snow patches
moon-peak-trek.description: The push toward Moon Peak begins in the cold hour just after sunrise, when the scree slopes above camp are still firm from the overnight chill and easier to climb than they become later once the sun softens the loose surface. The route climbs steeply out of the cave's basin, following a rough line of cairns over broken rock that demands steady footwork rather than raw fitness, with hands needed on some of the steeper pulls. Roughly halfway up, the gradient briefly eases across a shoulder that gives the first uninterrupted view toward Indrahar Pass to the east, a useful landmark for orienting the wider Dhauladhar geography. The final approach to the Moon Peak ridge crosses a band of larger boulders and, depending on season, a short patch of consolidated snow that may need careful, kicked-step crossing. From the top, the view is genuinely expansive, the Kangra valley far below to the south, the folded ridgelines of the inner Dhauladhar to the north, and on exceptionally clear days a hint of higher snow peaks further into the range. Given the altitude, most groups keep summit time brief, snapping photos and eating a quick snack before beginning the descent while conditions remain stable, since cloud tends to build over the ridge from around midday onward. The way down follows the same line but feels notably different, with loosened scree making for careful, sliding steps rather than the firmer footing of the morning. Returning to Lahesh Cave by early-to-mid afternoon leaves time to rest and recover before the long descent to McLeod Ganj the following day.
highlights: Pre-dawn start for firmer scree conditions
Cairned route over broken rock and boulders to the ridge
Panoramic summit views spanning the Kangra valley and inner Dhauladhar
View toward Indrahar Pass from the halfway shoulder
tips: Start before sunrise while scree is still firm
Turn around by early afternoon before cloud builds over the ridge
Use trekking poles for the loose scree descent

moon-peak-trek.title: Lahesh Cave to McLeod Ganj
moon-peak-trek.trailType: Descending ridge and forest trail via Triund
moon-peak-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in McLeod Ganj
moon-peak-trek.description: With Moon Peak accomplished, the final day is dedicated entirely to descent, retracing the route down past the now-familiar landmarks of the past three days in a single long push back to civilization. The drop from Lahesh Cave to Triund goes quickly, gravity doing much of the work on legs that are noticeably more tired than they were on the way up, though the rockier upper sections still demand attention to avoid a twisted ankle this late in the trek. Triund appears almost welcoming after two nights at higher, harsher camps, and most groups stop for a final plate of Maggi and chai at one of the huts, a small ritual that seems to mark the unofficial end of the mountain portion of the trip. Below Triund the character of the walk shifts entirely, the open ridge giving way to shaded oak and deodar forest, with the temperature climbing noticeably as altitude drops. Local families and day-trippers become common again on this stretch, a reminder of how close McLeod Ganj actually is despite the sense of remoteness higher up. The final approach into town arrives through the same forest checkpost passed four days earlier, and the sudden noise and bustle of McLeod Ganj's lanes can feel almost overwhelming after days spent in near-silence above the treeline. Knees generally take the brunt of this long downhill day, so trekking poles are worth using even for those who skipped them on the ascent. A hot meal, a proper bed and a change of clothes await in town, closing out a trek that packs genuine high-altitude terrain into a compact four-day window.
highlights: Long descent retracing Triund and the forest checkpost
Final rest stop for Maggi and chai at Triund
Gradual transition from alpine ridge back to forest and village life
Return to McLeod Ganj's markets and cafes
tips: Use trekking poles; the descent is harder on knees than the climb up
Book a rest day in McLeod Ganj if joints feel strained
Carry snacks for the long single-push descent day

dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.title: Dharamshala to Rajera and the Lower Forest Camp
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.trailType: Village paths and pine forest trail
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.description: The trek begins not from McLeod Ganj's well-worn trailheads but from Rajera, a quiet farming village on the southern flank of the Dhauladhar that most Dharamshala visitors never see. A jeep track leads out of Dharamshala through terraced fields and past small brick kilns before narrowing into a footpath at the village edge, where local families still grow maize and pulses on the lower slopes. The trail climbs through mixed pine and oak forest that feels distinctly drier and more open than the moss-heavy forests around McLeod Ganj, a reflection of this side of the range catching less monsoon moisture. Small hamlets appear at intervals, their houses built in the same slate-roofed Gaddi style seen elsewhere in the region, and villagers are often out working the fields or tending goats even in the early afternoon heat. Water sources are less frequent on this stretch than on the northern trails, so bottles should be topped up at any village tap encountered along the way. By mid-afternoon the trail enters denser forest and the gradient steepens noticeably for the final push to the lower camp, a flat clearing used by shepherds moving flocks up toward the high pastures each summer. The Dhauladhar wall itself is not yet visible from camp, screened by the ridge above, which builds a sense of anticipation for the following day's approach. Evenings here are quiet compared to the busier northern trailheads, with birdsong and the occasional distant bark of village dogs the only sounds. This approach offers a genuinely different, less-trodden perspective on the range for trekkers who have already done the standard Triund routes.
highlights: Rarely-visited southern approach via Rajera village
Dry pine forest distinct from the moister northern Dhauladhar trails
Traditional Gaddi hamlets along the route
Shepherd camp clearing at the day's end
Quiet, uncrowded alternative to the Triund side
tips: Top up water at village taps; sources thin out on this side
Start early to avoid midday heat in the lower forest
Expect a quieter, less-crowded trail than the McLeod Ganj routes

dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.title: Lower Camp to the Dhauladhar Base Camp Meadow
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.trailType: Steep forest trail opening into alpine meadow
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.description: Leaving the shepherd clearing, the trail climbs hard through a belt of old-growth pine and fir, the forest floor carpeted with needles that muffle footsteps almost completely. Around the two-hour mark, the trees begin to thin abruptly, and the Dhauladhar's southern wall finally reveals itself in full, a near-vertical rampart of grey granite streaked with old snow in the gullies, dramatically closer and more immediate than the views typically seen from Triund or Naddi. The final stretch to base camp crosses a series of grassy benches, each slightly higher than the last, dotted with wildflowers in season and grazed by ponies belonging to local Gaddi herders who still use these meadows through summer. Base camp itself sits on the largest of these benches, a genuinely spectacular natural amphitheater with the rock wall rising directly behind and a wide, uninterrupted view south over the Kangra valley and, on clear days, the plains beyond. A small stream fed by snowmelt runs along the meadow's edge, cold and clean, and provides reliable water for the camp. Because this route doesn't aim to cross the range, camp here has a relaxed, almost holiday feel compared to pass-crossing treks, with plenty of daylight left after arrival for photography, short explorations, or simply sitting and watching the light change on the rock face through the afternoon. Weather can turn quickly at this elevation, with cloud sometimes rolling up from the valley in under an hour, so rain gear should stay accessible even on clear mornings. This meadow camp, rarely visited compared to the northern trailheads, offers arguably some of the most dramatic close-range mountain views in the entire Dharamshala region.
highlights: Dramatic close-range view of the Dhauladhar's southern rock wall
Grassy meadow benches grazed by Gaddi ponies
Reliable snowmelt stream at camp
Relaxed basecamp atmosphere without a pass to cross
Rarely-visited southern meadow camp
tips: Keep rain gear accessible; cloud can build within the hour
Use the relaxed afternoon for photography while light is good
Expect cold, clear nights with the rock wall looming close overhead

dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.title: Exploration Day: Viewpoint Spur Above Base Camp
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.description: With no need to push toward a pass, the third day is kept deliberately light, built around a day hike to a rocky spur above camp that gives an even more commanding view of the range than the meadow itself. The climb starts across boulder-strewn ground before picking up a faint, intermittent path used mainly by shepherds moving flocks between grazing grounds, and cairns are sparse enough that some care is needed to keep track of the return line. Height is gained quickly on this short but steep pull, and the effort pays off at the top with a genuinely rare vantage point, the full curtain of the Dhauladhar wall stretching in both directions, close enough to pick out individual gullies and rock ribs, with base camp reduced to a tiny green patch far below. On especially clear mornings, a hint of higher snow-clad peaks deeper in the range becomes visible over the nearer ridgeline, hinting at the scale of the wider Himalaya beyond this immediate wall. The descent back to camp is quick, and the remainder of the day is left free for rest, laundry at the stream, or simply lounging in the meadow watching light shift across the rock face as clouds drift past. Local Gaddi shepherds occasionally pass through camp with their flocks in the afternoon, often happy to chat if approached respectfully, offering a small window into a way of life that has continued largely unchanged on these slopes for generations. This unhurried extra day is what distinguishes the Dhauladhar base camp trek from faster-paced pass treks, giving trekkers genuine time to absorb the mountain rather than simply passing through.
highlights: Day hike to a rocky spur with an even wider Dhauladhar panorama
Glimpse of higher snow peaks beyond the immediate wall
Free afternoon for rest and photography in the meadow
Chance encounters with Gaddi shepherds and their flocks
tips: Keep track of the faint return path; cairns are sparse on the spur trail
Use the free afternoon to rest ahead of the long descent
Approach local shepherds respectfully if you'd like to chat

dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.title: Dhauladhar Base Camp to Dharamshala
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.trailType: Forest trail and village road descending through several villages
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in Dharamshala
dhauladhar-base-camp-trek.description: The return to Dharamshala is a single long descent, dropping nearly a mile in elevation over the course of the day as the trail retraces its way down through meadow, forest and village in reverse order. The grassy benches below camp pass quickly, and by mid-morning the route re-enters the pine and fir forest, cooler and shadier than the exposed meadows above but demanding careful footing on a trail made slippery by pine needles and loose soil. The shepherd clearing used as the first night's camp makes a natural lunch stop, familiar now after three days on the mountain, before the trail continues down into drier forest and eventually back among the terraced fields surrounding Rajera. Village life resumes gradually rather than suddenly on this southern approach, with small hamlets, grazing goats and working farmland reappearing well before Dharamshala itself comes into view. The final stretch follows the jeep track back into town, and the transition from mountain silence to Dharamshala's traffic and noise tends to arrive faster than expected. Knees and thighs take the brunt of this descent day, easily the most physically demanding of the trek despite requiring no technical skill, and trekking poles make a noticeable difference on the steeper forested sections. Looking back from the valley floor, the Dhauladhar wall that loomed so close over the past three days recedes into the familiar distant backdrop most Dharamshala visitors know, a useful reminder of just how close that dramatic scenery actually sits above the town. A hot meal and proper bed in Dharamshala mark a fitting end to a quieter, less-crowded alternative to the region's more famous trekking routes.
highlights: Long single-day descent through meadow, forest and farmland
Return through the traditional villages of the southern approach
Dramatic perspective shift as the Dhauladhar recedes to a distant backdrop
Fitting end to an uncrowded, alternative Dharamshala trek
tips: Use trekking poles for the steep, needle-covered forest sections
Start early to finish the long descent before afternoon heat peaks
Carry snacks; the shepherd clearing is the only reliable lunch stop

nag-dal-trek.title: Bir Billing to Rajgundha
nag-dal-trek.trailType: Forest jeep track and footpath through oak and rhododendron
nag-dal-trek.description: The approach begins at Bir, the village long associated with paragliding tourism, where colorful canopies drifting down from Billing's launch site are a near-constant sight on clear mornings. A winding road climbs from Bir up to Billing itself, and from the landing ground there the actual trekking begins, following a broad forest track used by both trekkers and the occasional supply mule heading toward Rajgundha. The path threads through a mixed forest of oak, rhododendron and occasional deodar, the canopy dense enough to keep the walk cool even as the day warms. Streams cross the trail at regular intervals, most easily forded on flat stones, and small clearings offer periodic views back toward Bir's patchwork of tea gardens spread across the valley floor far below. Rajgundha itself appears gradually rather than suddenly, a scattering of stone-and-wood houses set among terraced fields that mark the last permanently inhabited settlement on this route into the mountains. The village has increasingly turned to trekker homestays alongside traditional farming, and an evening spent here offers a good introduction to Gaddi hospitality, simple but generous meals, wood-fired warmth, and often a genuine curiosity about where the trek ahead will lead. Views from the village edge already hint at the scale of what's coming, with the ridgelines guarding the approach to the Saat Dal lakes visible in the distance. This first day, while not physically extreme, involves a genuine altitude gain from Bir's low valley floor, and pacing sensibly rather than rushing the forest track pays off in how the body handles the days ahead.
highlights: Paragliding views from Bir and the Billing launch ground
Forest trail through oak, rhododendron and deodar
Traditional Gaddi homestay culture in Rajgundha
First distant views of the Saat Dal lakes ridgeline
tips: Pace the forest walk; the elevation gain from Bir adds up quickly
Try a homestay meal in Rajgundha for genuine local hospitality
Cross streams on the flat stones rather than wading

nag-dal-trek.title: Rajgundha to Palachak Meadow
nag-dal-trek.description: Beyond Rajgundha's last terraced fields, the trail climbs into a transition zone where forest starts thinning into open meadow, the trees becoming progressively more stunted and wind-bent as height is gained. Wildflowers carpet the ground in the June-to-August window, with gentians and potentilla among the more common sights, while marmots occasionally whistle an alarm call from rocky outcrops before disappearing underground. Palachak, the day's destination, is a broad grazing meadow used by Gaddi shepherds moving flocks up toward higher pastures through the summer months, and their temporary stone-and-tarpaulin shelters are a common sight scattered across the grass. Camp here sits with an open view back down the valley toward Rajgundha and, on clear evenings, a hint of the Kangra plains far to the south catching the day's last light. The meadow's exposure means weather changes are more noticeable than in the sheltered forest below, and afternoon wind can pick up considerably even under otherwise clear skies. A small stream running through the meadow provides reliable water, though it's worth filling bottles before the shepherds' flocks pass through and disturb the clearer upper sections. This camp also marks a psychological shift in the trek, the comfortable villages are now behind, and the terrain ahead toward Nag Dal grows noticeably more rugged and remote. Evenings at Palachak are often social if other trekking groups or shepherd families are camped nearby, a last easy social stop before the quieter, higher ground of the following day. Rest here is worthwhile, since the push to the lake demands sustained climbing over increasingly rough ground.
highlights: Transition from forest to open alpine meadow
Seasonal wildflowers and resident marmots
Gaddi shepherd grazing camps at Palachak
Valley views back toward Rajgundha and the Kangra plains
tips: Fill water bottles before shepherd flocks pass through the stream
Expect stronger afternoon wind on the exposed meadow
Rest well; the push to Nag Dal is the trek's toughest day

nag-dal-trek.title: Palachak to Nag Dal Lake
nag-dal-trek.trailType: Boulder and scree slopes above the meadow line
nag-dal-trek.description: The climb out of Palachak leaves the last grazing meadows behind fairly quickly, the trail turning to a mix of scree and broken rock that demands steady, unhurried progress rather than speed. Nag Dal belongs to the loosely connected group of high alpine lakes locally known as the Saat Dal, or seven lakes, scattered across this pocket of the Dhauladhar's southern folds, and local legend holds that a serpent deity, or Nag, resides in its depths, the source of the lake's name and the small shrine occasionally found near its shore. The final approach crosses a rockier basin where snow patches can linger well into July in shadowed corners, and the lake itself appears without much warning, a startlingly still sheet of deep green-blue water framed by bare rock walls on three sides. Few trekking groups make it this far compared to the more heavily trekked passes further along the range, and the silence around the lake, broken only by occasional wind or the distant call of a Himalayan bird, has a particular quality that comes from genuine remoteness. Camp is pitched on a patch of relatively flat ground a short distance from the water's edge, chosen to avoid disturbing what is, for local shepherds, a place of some spiritual significance. Water from the lake's outflow stream is cold and clean, though the lake itself is generally left undisturbed out of respect for local belief. Temperatures drop sharply once the sun dips behind the surrounding ridgeline, and the evening light on the still water, catching the last color from the sky, is one of the trek's genuine highlights.
highlights: Nag Dal, one of the remote Saat Dal group of alpine lakes
Local legend of a serpent deity residing in the lake
Still, green-blue water framed by bare rock walls
Genuine remoteness with very few other trekking groups
tips: Respect the lake's spiritual significance; avoid entering the water
Use the outflow stream, not the lake itself, for drinking water
Layer up for a sharp temperature drop after sunset

nag-dal-trek.title: Nag Dal Exploration and Descent to Palachak
nag-dal-trek.trailType: Rocky ridge walk then descending scree and meadow
nag-dal-trek.description: With a full day allotted around Nag Dal, the morning is spent climbing a rocky spur above the lake's northern shore, gaining a few hundred feet to a vantage point that reveals, for the first time, the wider layout of the Saat Dal lake cluster, several smaller tarns visible in folds of the landscape that were completely hidden from the lake basin itself. Kali Kund and Lam Dal lie in a neighboring valley system rather than this one, but from this ridge their general direction can be pointed out, giving a useful sense of how these various alpine lakes relate to each other across the range's southern folds. The descent back to the lake is quick, and after a final look at the water and a last filling of bottles, the trek turns back down the way it came, retracing the scree and broken rock of the previous day's ascent. Moving downhill over this terrain is considerably faster than the climb up, though the loose surface still demands attention to avoid a rolled ankle this deep into remote terrain. Palachak's meadow, reached again by mid-to-late afternoon, feels noticeably warmer and greener after a night spent at the stark, rocky lake basin, and the return to grazing shepherds and open grass has an almost domestic feel after the silence above. This second night at Palachak allows for a more relaxed pace, since the remaining descent to Bir is comfortably achievable within a single day, and the evening is generally spent resting and reflecting on the lake visited only hours before.
highlights: Ridge viewpoint revealing the wider Saat Dal lake cluster
Distant orientation toward the Kali Kund and Lam Dal valleys
Fast descent back through the scree basin
Return to the greener, warmer Palachak meadow
tips: Fill bottles at the lake's outflow before starting the descent
Take care on the loose scree; ankles are vulnerable this deep into the trek
Use the relaxed evening at Palachak to recover before the final descent

nag-dal-trek.title: Palachak to Rajgundha and Bir Billing
nag-dal-trek.trailType: Meadow trail descending through forest to Billing
nag-dal-trek.accommodation: End of trek; guesthouse in Bir
nag-dal-trek.description: The final day retraces the entire route back through Rajgundha and down to Bir in a single long push, the meadow trail of the upper section giving way once again to shaded forest as the elevation drops. Rajgundha's terraced fields and homestays reappear by late morning, and many groups pause here for a final tea or simple meal before continuing the last stretch down to Billing. The forest descent below the village passes quickly, legs finding an easy downhill rhythm after four days of increasingly rugged terrain, and the first glimpses of paragliders drifting overhead near Billing signal the return to lower-altitude civilization. Reaching Billing's grassy landing ground, often busy with tourists watching or waiting for tandem flights, marks a sharp contrast to the silence of Nag Dal just a day's walk behind. The final stretch down to Bir follows the road, and most trekkers use this last hour to simply unwind, knowing the physical demands of the trek are essentially finished. Looking back up toward the ridgeline from the valley floor, it's difficult to pick out exactly where Nag Dal sits hidden among the folds of rock, a fitting final reminder of just how tucked-away that lake really is relative to the busier trails elsewhere in the Dhauladhar. Bir's cafes and guesthouses offer a comfortable end to the trip, and a celebratory meal is a fitting close to a trek that visits one of the least-known alpine lakes in the entire region. Transport onward to Dharamshala or elsewhere in Kangra is readily available from Bir's main road.
highlights: Full descent from alpine meadow back to forest and village
Return to Rajgundha's homestays for a final tea stop
Paragliders overhead near Billing signaling the return to lower altitude
End of trek in Bir with onward transport options
tips: Pause in Rajgundha for a final meal before the last descent
Watch tandem paragliding flights at Billing if time allows
Arrange onward transport from Bir in advance during peak season

kali-kund-trek.title: Barot to Lohardi Base Camp
kali-kund-trek.trailType: River trail along the Chuhar Nullah through forest
kali-kund-trek.description: Barot's identity as a quiet trout-fishing town built around the Uhl river's hydel project gives way quickly to wilder terrain once the trail leaves the last guesthouses behind, following the river's tributary, the Chuhar Nullah, upstream into the forest. The path stays close to the water for much of the morning, crossing on log bridges at a couple of points where the stream narrows, and anglers are sometimes spotted working the pools even this far from town. Forest here is a mix of deodar and blue pine, with the understory notably lush thanks to the moisture carried up the valley, and the trail underfoot is often damp and root-tangled rather than dusty. As the valley narrows, the gradient increases, and the last cultivated terraces give way entirely to unbroken forest by early afternoon. Lohardi, the base camp for the push toward Kali Kund, sits on a relatively flat bench above the stream, chosen by generations of shepherds and, more recently, trekking groups, for its combination of flat ground, reliable water and reasonable shelter from wind. From camp, the higher ridgeline guarding Kali Kund is visible through gaps in the canopy, though the lake itself remains hidden until much closer. This valley receives fewer visitors than the Bir-Rajgundha corridor to the east, and the relative quiet is one of its main appeals for trekkers seeking Dhauladhar scenery away from the busier routes. Evening temperatures drop quickly once the sun leaves the narrow valley, and camp routines settle in early. Wood smoke from the cook fire tends to hang low in the still evening air here, and headlamps stay close at hand since the transition from dusk to full dark happens quickly beneath the steep valley walls.
highlights: Trout-fishing town of Barot as trek start point
Riverside forest trail along the Chuhar Nullah
Log-bridge stream crossings through deodar and blue pine
Quiet, lesser-visited approach compared to the Bir-Rajgundha corridor
tips: Watch for slippery log bridges over the stream
Fill water bottles at camp; the trail thins out water sources higher up
Expect an early, cold evening in the narrow valley

kali-kund-trek.title: Lohardi to Kali Kund Lake
kali-kund-trek.trailType: Steep ridge and boulder slopes above the treeline
kali-kund-trek.description: Above Lohardi the forest thins rapidly, and the trail turns into a sustained climb up a rocky spur that gains most of the day's considerable elevation in a series of steep, switchbacked pulls. This is the physical heart of the trek, and the effort is real, legs and lungs both work hard as the trees fall away and the terrain turns to open scree and broken rock, with the last hardy juniper bushes clinging to sheltered pockets before disappearing entirely. Cairns mark a rough line up the spur, though the going is largely a matter of picking the most stable-looking route through the boulders underfoot. As the ridge crest nears, the basin holding Kali Kund comes into view below, a striking body of near-black water framed by steep rock walls, the lake's name, meaning dark pond, reflects the unusually deep, shadowed color of its surface even under bright midday sun. Local belief holds the lake sacred, associated with the wider Lam Dal pilgrimage tradition practiced by shepherd communities in this part of the Dhauladhar, and small stone cairns or offerings are sometimes left near the shore by visiting herders. Camp is set a respectful distance from the water on a patch of relatively flat ground, with views back down the valley toward Barot receding far below. The cold at this elevation sets in quickly once the sun drops behind the surrounding ridge, and clear skies here typically deliver an exceptional, undiluted view of the night sky given the total absence of any artificial light for many kilometers in every direction.
highlights: Steep spur climb from forest to alpine scree
Kali Kund's distinctive dark, deep-colored water
Connection to the Lam Dal shepherd pilgrimage tradition
Exceptional night sky viewing with zero light pollution
tips: Pace the steep switchbacks; this is the trek's hardest climbing day
Camp respectfully away from the water's edge
Bring warm layers for a genuinely cold, clear high-altitude night

kali-kund-trek.title: Kali Kund to Barot: Long Descent
kali-kund-trek.trailType: Retracing ridge, boulder and forest trail in a single descent
kali-kund-trek.accommodation: End of trek; guesthouse in Barot
kali-kund-trek.description: The return to Barot is done in a single committed push, retracing the steep spur back down through scree and boulder before the welcome sight of the first juniper bushes signals the return of some vegetation. This descent is long rather than technical, but the sustained downhill takes a real toll on knees and ankles, particularly through the upper rocky sections where loose stones shift underfoot with every step. Lohardi's flat bench arrives by late morning for most groups, offering a natural break point and a chance to refill water before the second half of the descent through forest. Below the base camp, the trail rejoins the Chuhar Nullah and follows it back down through deodar and blue pine, the sound of running water a constant companion for the rest of the day. The temperature climbs noticeably as the valley widens and altitude drops, and by the time the first terraced fields around Barot reappear, the crisp cold of the previous night at Kali Kund feels like a distant memory. Trout-fishing enthusiasts occasionally appear working the river's pools on this lower stretch, a reminder of Barot's other identity as a low-key angling destination. Reaching Barot itself in the late afternoon completes a compact but genuinely demanding three-day trek, one that reaches a strikingly atmospheric alpine lake without the extended approach required by treks further east toward Bir and Rajgundha. A hot meal, the sound of the Uhl river, and a proper bed make for a satisfying close after a long final day on the trail.
highlights: Long single-day descent from alpine lake to riverside town
Return through juniper, deodar and blue pine forest zones
Chuhar Nullah riverside trail on the lower stretch
Compact three-day format reaching a genuinely remote lake
tips: Use trekking poles on the steep upper scree descent
Refill water at Lohardi before the second half of the descent
Allow a rest day in Barot afterward; the final day is demanding

lahesh-cave-trek.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund: Overnight Trek Start
lahesh-cave-trek.trailType: Stone-stepped forest trail through cedar and oak
lahesh-cave-trek.description: This short overnight trek starts at the same trailhead used by countless day-trippers heading to Triund, but the pace and mindset differ from a single-day outing since there's no rush to be back down by evening. The path climbs through cedar and oak forest on a well-graded trail that has been improved over the years with stone steps in the steeper sections, making it one of the more comfortable ascents in the Dhauladhar despite the steady elevation gain. Gallu temple, roughly a third of the way up, is a popular rest stop where a small stall usually sells tea and biscuits to passing trekkers, and the shade here is welcome before the trail opens into more exposed terrain above. Groups of local school trekking parties and international backpackers mix freely on this stretch, giving the walk a sociable character quite different from the quieter trails elsewhere in the region. As the forest thins, magpies and occasional Himalayan griffons can be spotted riding thermals along the ridge, and the first uninterrupted views of the snow-streaked Dhauladhar face appear well before Triund itself comes into sight. Arriving at Triund's broad grassy shoulder in the early-to-mid afternoon leaves ample time to settle into camp, wander the ridge, or simply watch the paragliders that occasionally drift down from higher launch points. Sunset here draws nearly everyone out of their tents, cameras out, as the light turns the Kangra valley below into a soft golden haze while the peaks above hold the color longest. This is deliberately an easy first day, building a comfortable base before the shorter, steeper stretch to Lahesh Cave tomorrow.
highlights: Well-graded stone-stepped trail through cedar and oak
Gallu temple tea stop
Lively mix of local and international trekkers
Golden-hour views over the Kangra valley from Triund
tips: Enjoy the relaxed pace; there's no need to rush this easier first day
Watch for paragliders drifting past camp in the late afternoon
Arrive early enough to claim a good tent spot with a view

lahesh-cave-trek.title: Triund to Lahesh Cave: Overnight Under the Rock
lahesh-cave-trek.trailType: Rocky ridge trail with the last stunted birch
lahesh-cave-trek.description: The stretch from Triund to Lahesh Cave is short in distance but noticeably different in character, trading Triund's open grassy shoulder for a narrower, rockier ridge trail that feels more genuinely mountainous with every step. Loose stone and the occasional larger boulder require more careful footing than the previous day, and the last scattered clumps of stunted birch mark the effective end of any real vegetation. Lahesh Cave itself is the trek's main draw for this stage, a substantial natural rock overhang large enough to shelter a group of tents entirely from wind and any rain, a novelty that many trekkers specifically come to experience rather than simply pass through. Arriving by early afternoon leaves plenty of time to explore the immediate surroundings, including a short scramble to a small rise just above the cave that offers a clear look toward Indrahar Pass and the jagged skyline beyond, a preview of terrain that lies well beyond this particular trek's scope. Because there's no onward pass to cross, the atmosphere at camp tends toward relaxed exploration rather than logistics and planning, and many groups spend the afternoon simply enjoying the novelty of camping under an open rock roof rather than pushing further. Temperatures drop sharply after sunset, colder than most trekkers expect after the relatively mild night at Triund, and condensation can build inside the cave if tents aren't pitched with some airflow in mind. A small stream a short walk below camp provides water, running clear but very cold. Nights here, largely free of light pollution, offer some of the clearest stargazing available on any short trek out of McLeod Ganj.
highlights: Overnight camping beneath the natural Lahesh Cave rock overhang
Rocky ridge trail with the region's most dramatic close-range views
Optional scramble to a rise overlooking Indrahar Pass
Exceptional stargazing with minimal light pollution
tips: Pitch tents with some airflow to avoid condensation inside the cave
Layer up; nights here are colder than Triund
Fill water bottles at the stream below camp before dark

lahesh-cave-trek.title: Lahesh Cave to McLeod Ganj
lahesh-cave-trek.trailType: Descending ridge and forest trail via Triund and Gallu temple
lahesh-cave-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in McLeod Ganj
lahesh-cave-trek.description: The return to McLeod Ganj retraces both of the previous days' trails in a single downhill push, and most trekkers are surprised by how quickly familiar ground passes when gravity is doing most of the work. The rocky ridge back to Triund goes by fast, though the loose stone underfoot still calls for attention even on tired legs eager to get moving. A final pause at Triund is almost obligatory, whether for a last cup of chai at one of the tea huts or simply to look back one more time at Lahesh Cave, now a small dark notch on the ridge above. From there, the forest trail down through oak and cedar feels considerably easier than the climb up two days earlier, and the stone steps near Gallu temple, tiring on the way up, are almost pleasant heading down. The bustle of McLeod Ganj's lanes returns gradually as the trail nears the town, day-trippers heading up passing groups heading down in a steady two-way flow typical of this popular route. Arriving back by early-to-mid afternoon leaves time for a proper shower, a real meal, and some souvenir shopping in the market before evening. This short, accessible trek delivers a genuine overnight cave-camping experience and some of the best close-range Dhauladhar views available anywhere near Dharamshala, all within a manageable three-day window suited to trekkers without extensive high-altitude experience. Many use it as a confidence-building step before attempting longer routes like Indrahar Pass or Moon Peak later in their trip.
highlights: Fast, gravity-assisted descent retracing both prior days
Final tea stop at Triund overlooking Lahesh Cave
Good confidence-building trek before attempting longer Dhauladhar routes
Return to McLeod Ganj's markets by early afternoon
tips: Stay attentive on loose stone even on the easier descent
Treat this as a good warm-up before attempting Indrahar Pass or Moon Peak
Save time for the market in McLeod Ganj on return

gaj-pass-trek.title: Bir Billing to Rajgundha: Expedition Start
gaj-pass-trek.trailType: Jeep track and forest footpath toward the last village before the pass
gaj-pass-trek.description: Loading mules with a week's worth of food and gear at Bir Billing's edge sets the tone for what is a genuine pass-crossing expedition rather than a casual walk, and the difference is obvious from the outset in the sheer bulk of supplies being organized for the trail ahead. The jeep track above Billing's paragliding ground gives way to footpath within the first hour, climbing through forest that alternates between open rhododendron stands and denser patches of oak, with intermittent views back over Bir's tea gardens and the wider Kangra valley beyond. Local porters, many of whom have crossed Gaj Pass numerous times over the years, tend to set a brisk, sustainable pace that's worth matching rather than fighting against on this first day. Rajgundha, reached by mid-afternoon, functions as the last proper village before the trek turns fully remote, and its homestays are used here specifically to top up on fresh supplies and local information about current pass conditions, which can vary considerably depending on recent snowfall even in peak season. Evening conversations with villagers often center on the state of the route ahead, since Gaj Pass sees far fewer crossings each year than the more established Bir-to-Bara Bhangal corridor via Thamsar, and up-to-date knowledge is valuable. The mood at camp carries a slight edge of anticipation given the technical crossing still days away, distinct from the relaxed, holiday atmosphere typical of shorter treks starting from the same trailhead. Rest is prioritized, since the days ahead only get more demanding from here.
highlights: Mule-loading and expedition-style preparation at Bir Billing
Forest trail through rhododendron and oak toward Rajgundha
Local porter knowledge on current Gaj Pass conditions
Last village stop before the trek turns fully remote
tips: Match the porters' sustainable pace rather than rushing
Gather current pass-condition information from Rajgundha villagers
Top up on any last supplies before leaving the village

gaj-pass-trek.title: Rajgundha to Panihar Meadow
gaj-pass-trek.description: Leaving Rajgundha's fields behind, the trail climbs into a broad, open meadow system known locally as Panihar, gaining height steadily through a transition zone where forest gives way first to scattered rhododendron scrub and then to genuinely open alpine grass. This stretch offers some of the trek's best wildflower displays in season, with primulas and anemones dotting the meadow in dense patches through much of the summer. Streams braid across the flatter sections of the meadow, occasionally requiring a short detour to find a dry crossing, and the water here, fed directly by snowmelt from the peaks ahead, is some of the cleanest available on the entire route. Panihar itself sits at a natural bottleneck in the valley, used by shepherds as a staging meadow before pushing their flocks higher toward the pass in the height of summer, and their temporary shelters are sometimes visible scattered across the grass. From camp, the route toward Gaj Pass becomes visible for the first time as a notch in the ridgeline ahead, still distant but unmistakably the objective now driving the trek's pace and planning. Afternoon cloud tends to build over the surrounding peaks by early afternoon, occasionally bringing brief showers even in otherwise settled weather, and the guide typically uses these conditions to gauge how the pass crossing itself might play out in the coming days. This is generally the last comfortable, grassy camp before the terrain turns rockier and more demanding, and evenings here are spent resting deliberately ahead of the harder days to come.
highlights: Panihar's wildflower-rich alpine meadow
Braided snowmelt streams with excellent water quality
Shepherd staging camps below the pass
First clear sighting of the Gaj Pass notch on the ridgeline
tips: Detour to dry crossings rather than wading through braided streams
Watch afternoon cloud buildup as an indicator of pass conditions ahead
Rest deliberately; the grassy comfort of this camp won't return until after the pass

gaj-pass-trek.title: Panihar to Gaj Pass High Camp
gaj-pass-trek.trailType: Boulder and scree slopes below the pass
gaj-pass-trek.description: Above Panihar the meadow narrows and steepens quickly into a boulder-strewn approach valley, the grass and wildflowers of the previous day replaced almost entirely by broken rock and increasingly persistent patches of old snow tucked into shaded gullies. The climb to high camp is short in distance but genuinely taxing, gaining considerable height over ground that offers little consistent footing, and pacing becomes noticeably more deliberate as the altitude starts to be felt in earnest. Gaj Pass itself comes into full view from partway up this stretch, a distinct saddle in the ridge flanked by steeper rock on both sides, looking considerably more serious up close than it did from the meadows below. High camp is a small, exposed platform chosen mainly for being the highest reasonably flat ground before the final approach to the pass, with limited shelter from wind that tends to pick up noticeably in the late afternoon and evening. Water at this camp comes from a direct snowmelt trickle, requiring some patience to collect enough for the group, and it runs cold enough to sting fingers within seconds. The guide typically uses the evening to brief the team thoroughly on the crossing plan for the next day, including the importance of an early start to cross the pass before the sun softens any snow on the far side and while wind conditions remain calmer. Sleep at this altitude tends to be lighter and more broken than at lower camps, and most groups turn in early regardless, aware of the demanding day that lies immediately ahead.
highlights: Boulder and old-snow approach valley below the pass
First close, serious-looking view of the Gaj Pass saddle
Exposed high camp on the last flat ground before the crossing
Detailed crossing briefing for the early pass-crossing start
tips: Start collecting water early; the snowmelt trickle at camp is slow
Sleep early despite light rest; tomorrow's crossing demands full energy
Secure tents well against strengthening evening wind

gaj-pass-trek.title: Cross Gaj Pass to the Chamba-Side Camp
gaj-pass-trek.trailType: Steep scree and snow crossing then descending scree into the Chamba-side valley
gaj-pass-trek.accommodation: Tented camp on the Chamba side
