gaj-pass-trek.description: The alarm sounds well before dawn, and the team is moving up the final approach to Gaj Pass while the snow underfoot is still firm from the overnight freeze, a genuinely important consideration since the same slopes turn soft and unstable once direct sun hits them later in the morning. The climb to the saddle is steep and sustained, alternating between scree and short snow patches that may require careful kick-stepping depending on the season's snow cover, and the final approach to the top narrows enough that the group generally moves in a single careful file. Reaching the pass itself delivers an abrupt and dramatic change of scenery, behind lies the familiar Kangra side of the range, while ahead, the folded ridgelines and hidden valleys of the Bhangal and Chamba side unfold in a landscape few trekkers ever see. The descent from the pass is, if anything, more demanding than the climb, dropping steeply over loose scree and occasional snow that has already begun softening by the time the group starts down, requiring careful, controlled steps rather than any rushing. Lower down, the terrain eases into rougher alpine grass and scattered rock, and the first camp on the Chamba side appears in a sheltered hollow well below the pass, a considerable relief after the exposure of the crossing itself. This is easily the longest and most demanding day of the entire trek, and arriving at camp by late afternoon or early evening, tired but having successfully crossed a genuinely remote Dhauladhar pass, marks the expedition's clear high point. Boots and socks come off the moment tents are up, and the guide checks everyone's feet and hands carefully for early signs of cold injury after such a long day spent largely on snow and wet rock.
highlights: Pre-dawn crossing of Gaj Pass on firm overnight snow
Dramatic first views of the hidden Bhangal/Chamba side valleys
Steep, sustained descent over scree and softening snow
Longest, most demanding day of the entire trek
tips: Start in the dark to cross before the snow softens
Move in single file on the narrow final approach to the saddle
Use controlled, deliberate steps on the loose, softening descent

gaj-pass-trek.title: Chamba-Side Camp to Holi Village
gaj-pass-trek.trailType: Descending forest trail along a Chamba-side stream
gaj-pass-trek.description: With the pass now behind the group, the trail settles into a long but far less demanding descent, following a stream that gathers volume steadily as smaller tributaries join it on the way down toward the Chamba valley floor. Forest reappears gradually, birch and fir giving way eventually to denser mixed woodland as altitude drops, a welcome change after the stark rock and snow of the previous two days. Small clearings along the stream occasionally reveal distant views of Bhangal-side ridgelines still holding snow, a lingering reminder of the terrain just crossed, even as the trail itself grows progressively gentler and greener. Holi, the day's destination, is a proper Chamba-side village with terraced fields, apple orchards and a noticeably different architectural style from the Kangra-side hamlets seen earlier in the trek, reflecting the cultural shift that comes with crossing into a new valley system entirely. Villagers here are generally curious about trekkers arriving from the Gaj Pass direction, since far more traffic through Holi comes from the Chamba road side than from the remote pass behind the group. A homestay or simple guesthouse in the village offers the first proper roof and real bed in five days, a small luxury that feels considerable after the exposed camps of the pass crossing. Evening here typically involves restocking food where possible and confirming onward transport arrangements for the final push toward Chamba town, since Holi itself sits on a reasonably motorable road connecting further into the district. Porters who joined the trek back in Rajgundha often part ways here, and a proper thank-you meal shared with them before they head back over the pass is a fitting way to mark the end of the expedition's hardest stretch.
highlights: Long, gentle descent following a growing Chamba-side stream
Return of dense mixed forest after two days above the treeline
Holi village's distinct Chamba-side architecture and orchards
First proper roof and bed since the pass crossing
tips: Enjoy the easier gradient; this is a recovery day after the pass
Confirm onward transport from Holi the evening you arrive
Ask villagers about restocking food supplies if needed

gaj-pass-trek.title: Holi to Chamba: Final Trek and Road Transfer
gaj-pass-trek.trailType: Village road and trail to the roadhead, then vehicle transfer
gaj-pass-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in Chamba/Bharmour
gaj-pass-trek.description: The final stage of the trek is short and largely a formality compared to the days that preceded it, following a quiet village road out of Holi past more orchards and terraced fields toward the wider Chamba valley road network. Traffic increases gradually, small shared jeeps and the occasional local bus passing on the improving road surface, a clear sign of returning to more accessible terrain after nearly a week spent in genuinely remote country. The walk itself, where the road narrows enough to still count as trekking, passes through a couple of smaller villages where daily life continues much as it has for generations, largely undisturbed by the trickle of trekkers coming over Gaj Pass each season. Looking back from lower in the valley, the high ridgeline holding the pass is usually still visible, a useful point of reflection on the distance and terrain covered since leaving Bir Billing days earlier. From the roadhead, a vehicle transfer covers the remaining distance into Chamba town itself, where the trek officially concludes amid a noticeably different cultural and architectural atmosphere from Dharamshala, older temples, a different dialect, and a slower, less touristy pace generally. Trekkers completing Gaj Pass often note it as one of the more genuinely remote crossings available in the wider Dhauladhar system, seeing a fraction of the traffic of routes like Indrahar Pass despite comparable difficulty. A final group meal in Chamba, with hot food and a proper bed, makes for a satisfying and well-earned close to a demanding six-day expedition across one of the range's lesser-known passes.
highlights: Easy final walk through orchards and Chamba valley villages
Vehicle transfer completing the journey into Chamba town
Final look back at the Gaj Pass ridgeline from the valley
Recognition of Gaj Pass as a genuinely remote, lesser-crossed route
tips: Arrange the roadhead-to-Chamba vehicle transfer a day ahead if possible
Explore Chamba town's temples once the trek concludes
Compare notes with locals; few trekkers cross this particular pass each year

chobia-pass-trek.title: Bir to Rajgundha: The Rare Crossing Begins
chobia-pass-trek.description: Chobia Pass sees so few crossings each year that even arranging a local guide familiar with current route conditions takes some advance effort, and this reality sets a noticeably more serious tone from the very first steps out of Bir. The trail climbs past Billing's paragliding strip and into forest that quickly feels more overgrown and less trodden than the equivalent stretch on more popular Dhauladhar routes, with fallen branches and encroaching undergrowth suggesting infrequent foot traffic. Rhododendron thickets crowd the path in places, forcing single-file movement, while gaps in the canopy offer occasional views back toward Bir's paragliding gliders circling far below. Rajgundha, reached in the afternoon, is the last village with any real infrastructure before the route heads into genuinely wild country, and information gathered here about snow conditions on Chobia carries real weight in the days-long planning ahead. Unlike the steadier stream of trekkers passing through en route to Rajgundha meadows or Bara Bhangal, groups headed for Chobia draw curious questions from villagers, since the pass remains something of a local rarity even among experienced Gaddi shepherds. Loads are checked carefully this evening, with extra attention given to technical gear that won't be needed until several days later but must be carried the entire way regardless. The relative obscurity of this crossing compared to Thamsar or Indrahar is part of its appeal for experienced trekkers seeking a genuinely off-the-radar Himalayan crossing, and that sense of venturing into rarely-covered ground is already palpable on this very first day.
highlights: One of the Dhauladhar's rarest, least-crossed passes
Overgrown, lightly-used forest trail beyond Billing
Local curiosity in Rajgundha about the unusual route ahead
Genuine off-the-radar Himalayan crossing experience
tips: Arrange a guide with recent Chobia-specific route knowledge in advance
Gather current snow-condition intel from Rajgundha villagers
Check all technical gear now; there's no resupply once you continue

chobia-pass-trek.title: Rajgundha to Chota Thach Meadow
chobia-pass-trek.trailType: Alpine meadow and old glacial moraine
chobia-pass-trek.description: Beyond Rajgundha's last cultivated fields, the trail climbs into increasingly wild meadow country, and the name Chota Thach, literally small pasture, undersells what is actually a sprawling, undulating expanse of high grassland stretching well beyond the day's camp in several directions. Rhododendron scrub thins into scattered juniper as height increases, and the first proper moraine debris, evidence of past glacial activity in this valley, begins appearing among the grass in patches of grey rubble. Streams cut across the meadow at multiple points, occasionally deep enough to require careful stepping-stone crossings rather than a simple wade, particularly in the early season when snowmelt runs highest. Wildlife sightings are relatively common on this stretch, with musk deer occasionally spotted bounding away through the grass and Himalayan monal sometimes glimpsed near the meadow's rockier fringes. Chota Thach itself, chosen as the day's camp for its flat, sheltered ground within a slight fold of the valley, offers the last reliably comfortable camping before terrain turns rockier and more exposed on the approach to Chobia's base camp. From here, the route ahead climbs visibly toward a distant notch in the skyline, the pass itself still two days off but already the clear focus of conversation and planning. Weather at this elevation can shift quickly, and afternoon cloud cover is common even on otherwise settled days, occasionally accompanied by brief hail showers that pass as quickly as they arrive. This camp marks a meaningful transition point, the last of the trek's genuinely gentle, grassy terrain before the harder ground begins in earnest.
highlights: Sprawling high-altitude meadow at Chota Thach
Evidence of past glacial moraine scattered through the grass
Musk deer and Himalayan monal sightings
Last comfortable, sheltered camp before rockier terrain begins
tips: Use stepping stones for stream crossings; wading can be deceptively deep
Watch for brief hail showers on otherwise clear afternoons
Enjoy the grassy camp; ground gets rockier from here onward

chobia-pass-trek.title: Chota Thach to Chobia Base Camp
chobia-pass-trek.description: The valley narrows and steepens considerably above Chota Thach, the grass thinning into a boulder field that marks the beginning of a much more serious approach toward the pass still two days distant. Route-finding through the moraine requires real attention, with the general line marked only by occasional cairns that are easy to lose track of among the jumbled grey rock. A glacial stream, milky with suspended rock flour, runs alongside much of this stretch, and its crossing points shift from year to year depending on how the ice above has retreated or advanced. Chobia's base camp sits on a rare patch of level ground tucked below a rocky spur that offers partial shelter from the wind that otherwise sweeps down from the pass with little obstruction. From camp, the pass itself becomes visible in full for the first time, a narrow gap flanked by steep rock and, depending on the season, considerable snow, looking notably more formidable up close than any description could suggest. This is also typically where the trek's mood shifts most noticeably, conversation turning from general trekking chat to specific, practical planning around timing, snow conditions and contingency options for the crossing ahead. Water sourced from the glacial stream needs time to settle before use, and the cold here is persistent even during daylight hours if cloud cover blocks the sun. Given the genuine remoteness of this base camp, the guide typically runs a thorough safety briefing this evening, covering both the acclimatization hike planned for the following day and the eventual pass crossing itself.
highlights: Serious moraine approach with cairned but faint route-finding
Glacial stream shifting course year to year
First full, formidable view of the Chobia Pass notch
Detailed safety briefing at this genuinely remote base camp
tips: Follow cairns carefully; the moraine route is easy to lose
Let glacial meltwater settle before filtering or drinking
Dress warm even in daylight if cloud blocks the sun

chobia-pass-trek.title: Acclimatization Day at Chobia Base Camp
chobia-pass-trek.description: A dedicated acclimatization day is essential at this altitude before attempting the pass, and the morning's short climb toward a rocky shoulder above camp serves the dual purpose of aiding the body's adjustment while also scouting the crossing route in daylight under calm conditions. The climb, though short, feels disproportionately tiring given the thin air, each step requiring more conscious effort than the same terrain would demand a few thousand feet lower. From the high point of this side excursion, the full approach to Chobia Pass can be studied in detail, the exact line through the final snow slopes, any visible crevassing or unstable-looking sections, and a rough sense of timing for the crossing day ahead. The guide uses binoculars where useful to assess snow conditions on the pass itself, information that directly shapes the decision on when to attempt the crossing over the following days. Descending back to base camp by early afternoon leaves the rest of the day free for rest, gear checks, and a final review of the technical equipment that will actually be used tomorrow or the day after, including crampons, ice axes and any rope work anticipated on the steeper snow sections. Symptoms of altitude sickness are monitored closely today, since Chobia's remoteness means evacuation options are limited compared to more frequently trekked passes, and any team member showing concerning symptoms is encouraged to consider turning back rather than risk a crossing while unwell. The relative isolation of this entire stretch of the trek reinforces just how much preparation and caution this particular pass demands compared to more established routes.
highlights: Scouting climb with a full view of the exact pass-crossing line
Guide's assessment of current snow and crevasse conditions
Final technical gear checks before the crossing
Close monitoring for altitude symptoms given limited evacuation options
tips: Move slowly on the scouting climb; the air is genuinely thin here
Use the free afternoon to double-check crampons and rope gear
Be honest about any altitude symptoms; Chobia offers few easy retreat options

chobia-pass-trek.title: Cross Chobia Pass to Kundi Got
chobia-pass-trek.trailType: Steep scree/snow pass crossing with a fixed-rope section, then long descent
chobia-pass-trek.description: The crossing of Chobia Pass begins in darkness, headlamps tracing the scouted line from the previous day's reconnaissance up firm, frozen snow that would become dangerously soft within a few hours of direct sun. The final approach steepens into a genuinely technical section, requiring careful cramponed steps and, depending on conditions, a fixed rope for security across the steepest section just below the saddle. Reaching the top of Chobia delivers one of the most remote, rarely-witnessed views in the entire Dhauladhar system, a tangle of unnamed peaks and hidden valleys stretching toward Chamba, utterly different from anything visible on the Kangra side just crossed. Time on the pass itself is kept brief given the exposure and lingering cold, and the descent begins almost immediately, dropping steeply over a mix of scree and softening snow that demands careful, controlled movement rather than any rushing. The Chamba-side terrain reveals itself gradually as height is lost, rockier and starker at first before easing into rougher alpine grass lower down. Kundi Got, the day's camp, sits in a sheltered hollow well below the pass, chosen specifically for the relative wind protection it offers after a day spent almost entirely exposed to the elements. Arriving here, often well into the afternoon or early evening given the day's sheer duration, marks unambiguously the hardest single day of the entire eight-day trek, both technically and in terms of sheer endurance. The sense of achievement at camp is considerable, tempered by genuine fatigue, and the team typically settles into an early, quiet night after one of the most demanding days most participants will have faced on any trek. Stoves run late into the evening melting extra snow for the next morning, since nobody has much appetite left for chores once dinner is finally served.
highlights: Pre-dawn crampon-and-rope crossing of Chobia Pass
Remote, rarely-seen view into the hidden Chamba-side valleys
Steep, sustained descent over scree and softening snow
Hardest single day of the entire eight-day trek
tips: Depart in full darkness to cross while snow is still frozen firm
Use crampons and the fixed rope section without hesitation near the saddle
Expect a very long day; pace energy reserves accordingly

chobia-pass-trek.title: Kundi Got to Deol Gran Village
chobia-pass-trek.trailType: Descending forest trail along a Chamba-side stream
chobia-pass-trek.description: With the pass now firmly behind the group, the day settles into a long but considerably gentler descent, following a stream down through increasingly vegetated terrain that feels like a slow return to normal after the stark exposure of the crossing. Alpine grass gives way to scattered birch, then denser fir forest, the transition marking real psychological relief alongside the physical one of easier ground underfoot. Deol Gran, a small Chamba-side settlement rarely visited by trekkers arriving from any direction other than over Chobia itself, appears as a scatter of stone houses along a terraced hillside, its residents visibly curious about a group arriving from a pass so few outsiders ever cross. Local hospitality here tends to be genuine and unhurried, less shaped by regular trekker traffic than villages further down the Chamba road network, and conversations with residents often reveal how rarely groups actually complete this particular route each season. A simple meal and, where available, a homestay roof offer welcome comfort after the previous day's exertion, a considerable upgrade from the tented camps of the past several nights. The valley's character here differs noticeably from the Kangra side left behind days earlier, different crops, different building styles, and a slightly different dialect all marking the cultural shift that comes with crossing a major Himalayan divide on foot rather than by road. Rest is prioritized this evening, with the bulk of the demanding terrain now behind the group and only gentler descent remaining before reaching more accessible roads over the following two days.
highlights: Gentle descent through birch and fir back to Chamba-side forest
Deol Gran, a settlement rarely visited except via Chobia Pass
Genuine, unhurried local hospitality distinct from busier trek routes
Noticeable cultural shift on the Chamba side of the divide
tips: Enjoy a homestay roof here if available; it's a real upgrade after the pass
Ask residents about the route's history; few groups cross Chobia each year
Rest well; the hardest terrain is now behind the group

chobia-pass-trek.title: Deol Gran to the Bharmour Road Village
chobia-pass-trek.description: The trail out of Deol Gran follows a well-used village path rather than anything resembling wilderness terrain, passing through a string of smaller hamlets connected by footbridges and terraced fields that mark a return to settled, everyday Himachal life. Traffic on the trail increases steadily through the day, with local farmers, schoolchildren and the occasional mule train sharing the same path that felt so isolated just two days earlier on the Chobia side. The Chamba valley opens up progressively as the trail descends, offering wide views over a patchwork of fields and scattered villages quite different in feel from the tighter, forested valleys of the Kangra side. Small temples and roadside shrines appear at intervals, often simple stone structures reflecting the particular devotional traditions of the Bharmour and Chamba region, historically significant as the old capital area of the Chamba princely state. By the time the trail nears its destination village, a proper motorable road becomes visible below, the first real road most of the group has seen in nearly a week, and the sense of returning to easy accessibility is considerable. The final village on this stretch has basic guesthouse accommodation and functions largely as a staging point for the short transfer into Bharmour or Chamba town the following day. Evening here is generally spent organizing gear, settling accounts with porters who will return to Bir via a different route, and reflecting on a crossing that very few trekkers in the region ever attempt, let alone complete successfully. Mobile signal returns properly for the first time in over a week at this village, and the sudden flood of messages is often the first real sign of how long the group has actually been out of contact.
highlights: Return to settled village life and everyday Himachal traffic
Wide views over the Chamba valley's patchwork farmland
Roadside shrines reflecting Bharmour-Chamba devotional traditions
First proper motorable road seen in nearly a week
tips: Settle accounts with porters this evening if they're returning separately
Arrange the next day's transfer into Bharmour or Chamba in advance
Take time to appreciate the shrines; this region has a distinct devotional history

chobia-pass-trek.title: Final Trek and Transfer to Bharmour/Chamba
chobia-pass-trek.trailType: Short road-adjacent trail to the vehicle pickup point
chobia-pass-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in Bharmour/Chamba
chobia-pass-trek.description: The final morning involves only a short walk along a quiet stretch of road-adjacent trail to reach a proper vehicle pickup point, a short and almost anticlimactic stretch of trekking compared to the sustained demands of the preceding week. Roadside stalls selling tea and snacks mark the trek's practical end point, a small but symbolic milestone after eight days that included one of the Dhauladhar's most remote and technically demanding pass crossings. The vehicle transfer from here into Bharmour, and onward to Chamba town if needed, takes a couple of hours along a winding mountain road that offers its own scenic rewards, passing temples, terraced hillsides and occasional glimpses back toward the high peaks the group crossed days earlier. Bharmour itself, historically significant as an old capital of the Chamba region, offers a handful of notable temples worth a look for anyone with time before onward travel, a fitting cultural coda to a trek that spent most of its duration in genuinely wild, uninhabited terrain. Reflecting on the trek from the comfort of a proper hotel room, the sheer contrast between the technical crossing of Chobia Pass and the quiet return through settled Chamba villages stands out as one of the trip's defining features. Very few trekking groups attempt this particular combination of Dhauladhar crossings each year, and completing it successfully ranks among the more serious achievements available to experienced trekkers in the wider Himachal Himalaya. A final debrief and celebratory meal in Bharmour or Chamba closes out an eight-day expedition that few will forget.
highlights: Short final trek stretch to the vehicle pickup point
Scenic mountain road transfer into historic Bharmour
Notable temples of Bharmour, an old Chamba capital
Completion of one of the Dhauladhar's rarest pass crossings
tips: Visit Bharmour's temples if time allows before onward travel
Book the vehicle transfer in advance to avoid delays at the roadhead
Celebrate; very few trekkers complete this particular crossing each year

jalsu-pass-trek.title: Barot to Multhan: Gateway to Chhota Bhangal
jalsu-pass-trek.trailType: Village road and forest trail through the Chhota Bhangal approach
jalsu-pass-trek.description: Barot's trout hatcheries and hydel infrastructure quickly disappear from view as the trail climbs out of the Uhl valley toward Multhan, the gateway village to Chhota Bhangal and, eventually, the approach to Jalsu Pass. The route follows a mix of forest track and quiet village road, passing through smaller hamlets where terraced barley and potato fields cling to slopes that seem almost too steep for cultivation. Deodar forest dominates the lower stretches, giving way to mixed oak and walnut as the trail gains height, and the smell of woodsmoke from village hearths often drifts across the path in the cooler morning hours. Multhan itself sits at a natural crossroads for this pocket of the Dhauladhar, historically a waypoint for shepherds and traders moving between the Kangra and Chamba sides of the range long before recreational trekking existed here. The village retains a distinctly traditional character, with slate roofs, carved wooden balconies on some of the older houses, and a pace of life that feels noticeably slower than the increasingly touristy villages further east near Bir. Homestays here offer a good introduction to Chhota Bhangal hospitality, and evening conversations with residents often touch on the seasonal migration patterns that still see families moving livestock up toward higher pastures each summer, a practice directly tied to the route the trek will follow over the coming days. Weather in this valley tends to be somewhat more sheltered than higher up, though afternoon cloud is still common, and the walk to Multhan makes for a solid, moderately paced first day before the terrain grows more demanding.
highlights: Gateway village of Multhan at the Chhota Bhangal crossroads
Traditional carved wooden architecture and slate roofs
Historic shepherd and trader route between Kangra and Chamba
Homestay introduction to Chhota Bhangal hospitality
tips: Ask about seasonal livestock migration; it shapes the whole route ahead
Try a Multhan homestay for a genuine cultural introduction
Expect a slower pace of life than the busier Bir-side villages

jalsu-pass-trek.title: Multhan to Jalsu Base Camp
jalsu-pass-trek.trailType: Forest trail and alpine meadow past shepherd grazing grounds
jalsu-pass-trek.description: Leaving Multhan's fields behind, the trail climbs steadily through forest that thickens noticeably compared to the drier lower valley, with ferns and moss covering much of the ground in a sign of the higher rainfall this pocket of Chhota Bhangal receives. Waterfalls, some barely a trickle and others surprisingly forceful depending on recent rain, cascade down the hillside at several points, and the trail crosses a couple of them on simple log or stone bridges. As the forest thins, the valley opens into a series of grazing meadows still actively used by Gaddi and Gaddan shepherd families, their flocks of sheep and goats a common sight moving between pastures through the middle of the day. Jalsu Base Camp sits at the upper edge of this meadow system, chosen for its flat ground and proximity to a reliable stream, with the pass itself visible as a distant gap in the ridgeline still a full day's climbing away. The camp offers wide views back down the valley toward Multhan and, on clear evenings, a hint of the more distant Kangra plains beyond. This is generally the last night with any real grass underfoot before the terrain above turns to rock and scree, and the shepherds occasionally camped nearby are usually happy to share informal updates on current conditions higher up the pass, information that carries real weight given how few official updates exist for a route this obscure. Rest here is worthwhile ahead of a genuinely demanding crossing day tomorrow, and most groups turn in early despite the relatively comfortable, grassy surroundings.
highlights: Forest waterfalls and log-bridge stream crossings
Active Gaddi and Gaddan shepherd grazing meadows
First distant view of the Jalsu Pass notch
Last comfortable grassy camp before rockier terrain begins
tips: Ask camped shepherds for informal updates on pass conditions
Cross waterfall bridges carefully, especially after recent rain
Rest well; tomorrow's crossing is the trek's most demanding day

jalsu-pass-trek.title: Cross Jalsu Pass to the Tundah Valley
jalsu-pass-trek.trailType: Scree and old snow crossing, then descent into the Tundah valley forest
jalsu-pass-trek.description: The push over Jalsu Pass starts early, the meadow grass of base camp quickly giving way to broken rock and, in most seasons, patches of old snow that linger in the pass's shaded upper approach well into summer. The climb is steady rather than technical in the classic mountaineering sense, but the sustained gradient and thinning air make for real effort, and the group typically settles into a slow, steady rhythm rather than pushing hard in short bursts. Reaching the pass itself reveals a striking change in landscape, behind lies the familiar Chhota Bhangal valley just climbed, while ahead, the folded hills and forested ridgelines of the Chamba side, part of the wider Tundah valley system, stretch out in a considerably different character of terrain, greener and less starkly alpine than the Kangra side. The descent from the pass drops quickly through scree and loose rock before easing into rougher grass and eventually scattered forest as the Tundah valley's characteristic dense woodland reappears. This side of the range receives noticeably more precipitation, evident in the lushness of the vegetation compared to the drier Kangra slopes left behind that morning. Camp for the night is pitched near a stream in a clearing within this transitional forest, a considerable change in scenery from the exposed high camp of the previous evening. This crossing, while less technically demanding than some of the range's more famous passes, still constitutes a genuinely long and tiring day, and arriving at camp with daylight to spare is generally considered a good outcome given the distance covered.
highlights: Early crossing of Jalsu Pass through lingering snow patches
Dramatic shift into the greener, wetter Tundah valley system
Descent through scree into increasingly lush Chamba-side forest
Genuinely long crossing day despite modest technical difficulty
tips: Settle into a slow, steady rhythm rather than pushing hard early
Expect noticeably wetter, lusher terrain on the Chamba side
Aim to reach camp with daylight to spare given the day's length

jalsu-pass-trek.title: Tundah Camp to Kugti Village
jalsu-pass-trek.trailType: Forest trail along a Budhil river tributary
jalsu-pass-trek.description: The trail below camp follows a tributary of the Budhil river down through increasingly dense forest, the transition from alpine terrain to a proper wooded valley now essentially complete after the previous day's crossing. Ferns, moss-covered boulders and a generally damper forest floor mark this side of the range as distinctly different from the drier Kangra slopes, and the sound of running water is almost constant throughout the day's walk. Small wooden footbridges span the stream at several points, some clearly older and more weathered than others, a sign of how long this route has served local communities moving between valleys long before any trekking interest existed here. Kugti, the day's destination, is a well-known village in this part of Chamba district, historically significant as a starting point for the challenging Kugti Pass trek that connects further into the Lahaul side of the Himalaya, giving the village a certain worldly familiarity with trekking groups despite its remoteness. Traditional wooden houses with elaborately carved facades line the village's narrow lanes, and a small temple dedicated to local deities sits near the village center, often visited by residents in the evening. Homestay accommodation here is relatively well-established compared to some of the smaller hamlets passed earlier in the trek, a byproduct of Kugti's status as a minor trekking hub in its own right. Evening in the village offers a good opportunity to rest properly after the demanding pass crossing, with a real bed and hot food marking a welcome change from the tented camps of the preceding nights.
highlights: Forest trail along a Budhil river tributary
Historic Kugti village, gateway to the separate Kugti Pass trek
Traditional carved wooden houses and a village temple
Established homestay comfort after the pass crossing
tips: Ask locals about the separate Kugti Pass trek if curious about onward routes
Check out the carved wooden house facades in the village lanes
Enjoy a proper bed here; it's a welcome change after tented camps

jalsu-pass-trek.title: Kugti to Hadsar and the Bharmour Road
jalsu-pass-trek.accommodation: End of trek; hotel in Bharmour
jalsu-pass-trek.description: The final day follows a well-established village trail down from Kugti toward Hadsar, a route walked daily by local residents and considerably more trafficked than anything encountered since crossing Jalsu Pass two days earlier. The Budhil river valley widens progressively, and terraced fields become more frequent, a clear sign of approaching the more populated lower reaches of this part of Chamba district. Hadsar itself sits close to a motorable road, and the walk here effectively marks the end of the trek's trekking portion, with a vehicle transfer covering the remaining distance into Bharmour or onward to Chamba town for those continuing their journey. Looking back up the valley from lower ground, the high ridgeline containing Jalsu Pass is often still visible on clear days, a fitting final view of the terrain crossed over the preceding days. This last stretch, while straightforward compared to the pass crossing itself, still offers pleasant walking through a valley with genuine cultural depth, given its historical role as a corridor connecting Chamba's interior villages to the outside world long before roads reached this far. Roadside tea stalls near Hadsar provide a welcome final stop before the transfer, and most groups take the opportunity to relax properly for the first time since leaving Multhan days earlier. Completing the Jalsu Pass trek offers a genuinely satisfying sense of having connected two distinct valley systems, Chhota Bhangal and the Budhil valley, entirely on foot, a route relatively few outside trekkers attempt compared to the region's more established crossings.
highlights: Well-trafficked final descent through the widening Budhil valley
Roadhead at Hadsar with vehicle transfer onward
Final views back toward the Jalsu Pass ridgeline
Satisfying sense of connecting two valley systems entirely on foot
tips: Relax at the Hadsar tea stalls before the vehicle transfer
Arrange onward transport to Bharmour or Chamba in advance
Look back for a last clear view of the pass on a good-weather day

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Bir Billing to Rajgundha: The Classic Route Begins
thamsar-pass-trek.trailType: Historic shepherd trail through rhododendron and deodar
thamsar-pass-trek.description: Thamsar Pass has long been considered the classic route into Bara Bhangal, and the walk begins amid the specific bustle of Bir Billing, where paragliders circle overhead against a backdrop of prayer flags strung between Tibetan settlement buildings, a scene that feels a world away from the remote valley the trek is ultimately headed toward. The trail climbs steadily above Billing's grassy takeoff strip into forest that alternates between rhododendron thickets and stands of tall deodar, with the path itself well-worn from generations of shepherd traffic supplemented in recent decades by a steady trickle of trekkers following the same historic line. Streams cross the route at regular intervals, most spanned by simple log bridges maintained informally by whichever village last needed them repaired. Rajgundha, arrived at by mid-to-late afternoon, carries genuine significance as the last settlement before the long uninhabited stretch toward Bara Bhangal, and its homestays have hosted trekkers attempting this crossing for decades, giving the village a level of trekking infrastructure unusual for its remote setting. Locals here are well accustomed to answering questions about the pass ahead, since Thamsar remains one of the more frequently attempted, if still genuinely serious, crossings in the wider Dhauladhar-Pir Panjal system. Evening conversations often turn toward the history of Bara Bhangal itself, a village so isolated it had no road access for decades and still relies on foot trails like this one for much of its connection to the outside world. This first day, while not physically extreme, sets the stage for a trek widely regarded as one of Himachal's classic multi-day crossings.
highlights: Bir Billing's paragliding and prayer-flag atmosphere at the start
Historic shepherd trail used for generations toward Bara Bhangal
Rajgundha's established trekking infrastructure as the last village
Introduction to the isolated history of Bara Bhangal
tips: Ask Rajgundha homestay hosts about current Thamsar Pass conditions
Use the well-maintained log bridges rather than attempting to wade streams
Treat this as the start of one of Himachal's genuine classic crossings

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Rajgundha to Palachak Meadow
thamsar-pass-trek.description: Above Rajgundha, cultivated fields give way within an hour or so to open meadow country that will characterize much of the trek's middle section, the trail climbing gradually rather than steeply as it follows the natural contour of the valley. This stretch is known for its wildflowers in the June-August window, with entire hillsides sometimes carpeted in blue and purple blooms that draw as much attention from trekkers as the mountain views themselves. Palachak meadow, the day's destination, is a broad grazing ground used seasonally by Gaddi shepherds moving flocks toward higher pastures, and their temporary shelters, simple stone walls topped with tarpaulin, are a common feature scattered across the grass through summer. The camp here offers sweeping views back down toward Rajgundha and, on especially clear evenings, a hint of the Kangra plains catching the last light far to the south. Streams braid gently across the meadow, providing reliable if occasionally muddy water sources, and the overall atmosphere at camp tends toward relaxed sociability, particularly if other groups attempting Thamsar are camped nearby, which is common during the main trekking season. This is generally regarded as one of the more pleasant camps on the entire route, comfortable and scenic before the terrain grows notably more rugged over the following two days. Weather remains relatively settled at this elevation most of the season, though afternoon cloud buildup is worth watching as an early indicator of conditions likely to be encountered higher up near the pass itself in the coming days.
highlights: Wildflower-carpeted meadow slopes in peak summer season
Seasonal Gaddi shepherd shelters scattered across Palachak
Sweeping views back toward Rajgundha and the Kangra plains
One of the most scenic, comfortable camps on the route
tips: Visit in June-August for the best wildflower displays
Expect a social camp atmosphere during peak Thamsar season
Watch afternoon cloud patterns as an early read on pass conditions

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Palachak to Thamsar Base Camp
thamsar-pass-trek.trailType: Rocky valley trail beside a glacial stream
thamsar-pass-trek.description: The trail beyond Palachak begins a more sustained climb, meadow grass gradually giving way to rockier ground interspersed with larger patches of scree as the valley narrows toward its head. This stretch offers the trek's first genuinely close views of the peaks flanking Thamsar Pass, their scale becoming increasingly apparent with every hour of upward progress. A glacial-fed stream accompanies much of the route, its water notably colder and cloudier than the clearer meadow streams of the previous day, a clear sign of proximity to permanent snow and ice higher up the valley. Thamsar Base Camp occupies one of the last reasonably flat, sheltered patches of ground before the terrain turns fully alpine, chosen deliberately for the protection it offers from wind that otherwise funnels down the valley from the pass with little obstruction. From camp, the pass itself remains hidden around a bend in the valley, though the general direction and scale of the terrain ahead is unmistakable from the surrounding peaks. This camp typically sees the trek's largest concentration of trekking groups during peak season, since virtually everyone attempting Thamsar uses this same staging point before the acclimatization day and eventual crossing. Evening temperatures drop noticeably compared to Palachak, and most groups spend the remaining daylight resting deliberately, aware that the following days demand considerably more sustained effort. Water from the glacial stream benefits from settling before filtering, given its heavy sediment content, and reliable weather information from groups who've recently crossed in either direction is often shared informally among camps here.
highlights: First close, scale-revealing views of the peaks flanking Thamsar Pass
Glacial stream marking proximity to permanent snow and ice
Sheltered staging camp used by nearly all Thamsar-bound groups
Informal weather and condition-sharing between camped trekking parties
tips: Let the glacial stream water settle before filtering
Rest deliberately; the following days are considerably more demanding
Chat with other camped groups for recent pass-condition updates

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Acclimatization Day and Thamsar Viewpoint Hike
thamsar-pass-trek.description: With the pass crossing still a day away, this stage is dedicated to acclimatization, built around a supervised hike up a rocky spur above base camp that gains meaningful altitude before returning to sleep at the lower elevation, following standard high-altitude practice. The climb, though not overly long, is a genuine test given the thinning air, with even fit trekkers finding their pace naturally slowing well before reaching the high point of the excursion. From the top of the spur, Thamsar Pass itself becomes visible for the first time as a clear notch in the ridgeline, along with a rough sense of the snow conditions likely to be encountered during tomorrow's crossing. This vantage point also offers a first glimpse toward the general direction of Bara Bhangal, still a full two days of walking away but psychologically significant as the trek's ultimate destination. Descending back to base camp by early afternoon leaves time for a proper rest, gear checks, and a detailed briefing from the guide covering the timing and logistics of the crossing ahead, including contingency plans should weather or snow conditions prove unfavorable. Symptoms of altitude are monitored carefully today, and anyone experiencing persistent headache, unusual fatigue or disturbed sleep is encouraged to communicate this clearly rather than push through silently, given how much more serious such symptoms can become at the altitudes reached tomorrow. This deliberate rest-and-acclimatize day is a key reason Thamsar has a reputation as a manageable, if serious, crossing compared to less carefully paced attempts on similar terrain elsewhere in the range.
highlights: Acclimatization climb with first clear view of the Thamsar Pass notch
Early glimpse toward the distant Bara Bhangal valley
Detailed crossing briefing and contingency planning
Careful altitude-symptom monitoring before the crossing
tips: Report any altitude symptoms honestly during this rest day
Use the spur-top view to mentally prepare for tomorrow's exact route
Prioritize proper rest over any temptation to explore further

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Cross Thamsar Pass to Riyali Camp
thamsar-pass-trek.trailType: Steep scree/snow pass crossing, then descent into the Bhangal-side valley
thamsar-pass-trek.description: The crossing begins in the pre-dawn cold, headlamps picking out a route already scouted the previous day, climbing over increasingly rocky and occasionally snow-covered ground toward the saddle itself. The final approach steepens noticeably, and depending on seasonal snow cover, crampons or at minimum careful kick-stepping may be needed across the last stretch before the top. Reaching Thamsar Pass delivers one of the most celebrated views in this part of the Himalaya, behind, the familiar Kangra-side terrain just climbed, and ahead, the dramatic, still largely roadless expanse of the Bara Bhangal valley system stretching into the distance, flanked by peaks that see relatively few visitors given the effort required to reach them. The descent from the pass drops steeply at first over scree and snow before gradually easing as the trail enters the upper reaches of the Bhangal side, vegetation slowly returning after the stark rock and snow of the crossing itself. Riyali, the day's camp, sits in a sheltered fold of the valley on this side, chosen for the relative wind protection it offers after a day spent largely exposed on the pass and its approaches. Arriving here, often in the mid-to-late afternoon given the day's considerable length, marks the trek's clear high point both literally and in terms of accomplishment, having successfully crossed one of the range's most storied passes. Fatigue by this point is genuine, and most groups settle into an early, satisfied rest, aware that the following day brings the reward of reaching Bara Bhangal itself, one of Himachal's most isolated inhabited villages.
highlights: Pre-dawn crossing of the celebrated Thamsar Pass
Dramatic first view into the roadless Bara Bhangal valley system
Steep descent through scree and snow to the Bhangal side
Sheltered Riyali camp after the trek's most demanding day
tips: Depart before dawn to cross while snow conditions are most stable
Use crampons if the final approach retains significant snow cover
Pace the steep descent carefully; legs are tired from the long climb

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Riyali to Bara Bhangal Village
thamsar-pass-trek.description: The final approach to Bara Bhangal follows the valley downstream through increasingly dramatic scenery, steep forested slopes rising on both sides of a river that gathers considerable volume as tributaries join from side valleys along the way. Bara Bhangal's isolation becomes tangible well before the village itself comes into view, the trail passing through long stretches with no sign of habitation beyond the occasional shepherd shelter, a reminder of just how remote this community remains even today. The village, when it finally appears, occupies a relatively flat shelf above the river, its houses built in the traditional Kangra-Chamba hybrid style reflecting the settlement's unusual position straddling both cultural and administrative boundaries between the two districts. Residents here have historically depended almost entirely on foot trails like Thamsar and the more difficult Kalihani Pass toward Manali for connection to the outside world, though a rough road has in recent years begun extending toward the village, slowly changing a way of life that persisted largely unchanged for generations. Walking through the village lanes offers a rare, genuine glimpse into this isolation, with residents generally welcoming toward trekkers who arrive having crossed the same passes their own families use for essential travel. A homestay or community guesthouse offers the first proper roof in several days, and the simple meals served here, often featuring local grains and vegetables grown in the village's limited but fertile fields, feel particularly satisfying after the sustained exertion of the pass crossing. Evening in Bara Bhangal, with the sound of the river and distant peaks catching the day's last light, ranks among the trek's most memorable experiences.
highlights: Arrival at Bara Bhangal, one of Himachal's most isolated villages
Traditional Kangra-Chamba hybrid architecture
Genuine local hospitality shaped by decades of foot-trail dependence
First proper roof and home-cooked meal after the pass crossing
tips: Walk respectfully through the village; it remains a working community, not a tourist site
Try a homestay meal featuring local grains and vegetables
Ask residents about their historical dependence on the Thamsar and Kalihani passes

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Rest and Exploration Day at Bara Bhangal
thamsar-pass-trek.description: A full day in Bara Bhangal allows for genuine recovery after six days of steady exertion, and the morning is typically spent exploring the village and its immediate surroundings at an unhurried pace rather than covering any significant new distance. A short walk to the upper fields above the village offers a comprehensive view of the settlement's layout, hemmed in by steep forested slopes on nearly every side, and makes clear just how limited the cultivable land here actually is despite supporting a community for generations. Conversations with villagers, many of whom speak with pride about their community's unusual history straddling Kangra and Chamba administrative lines, often reveal details about the seasonal migration many families still undertake, moving between Bara Bhangal and lower villages as winter approaches and the passes become impassable. Local shepherds, encountered tending flocks in nearby pastures, are typically happy to discuss current grazing patterns and, sometimes, offer informal insight into conditions on the return route over Thamsar in the days ahead. Simple exploration of the village's small temple and the layout of its traditional houses rounds out a day focused more on cultural immersion than physical trekking. Weather permitting, an optional short walk toward the river's edge below the village offers pleasant views and a chance to watch trout in the clearer pools, a small but memorable addition to the rest day. This built-in pause is widely considered essential preparation before the return crossing of Thamsar Pass, giving both body and mind proper recovery time after the demanding effort of reaching this remarkable, still largely inaccessible corner of Himachal.
highlights: Genuine rest day for recovery before the return crossing
Village-layout viewpoint from the upper fields
Conversations with residents about seasonal migration patterns
Optional riverside walk to watch trout in clear pools
tips: Use this day fully for rest; the return crossing is equally demanding
Ask about current conditions on the return route over Thamsar
Respect the village's working rhythm during your visit

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Bara Bhangal to Thamsar Base Camp: Re-Crossing the Pass
thamsar-pass-trek.trailType: Long return crossing over Thamsar Pass retracing the outbound route
thamsar-pass-trek.description: Leaving Bara Bhangal behind, the return journey begins by retracing the river valley trail back up toward Riyali, familiar ground now that eases the psychological load of what remains, overall, an extremely long and demanding day. The climb from Riyali back up toward Thamsar Pass follows the same line used during the crossing two days earlier, though tackling it from this direction, with several accumulated days of trekking already in the legs, tests endurance in a different way than the initial attempt. Snow conditions on the pass can differ noticeably from the outbound crossing depending on how the weather has shifted in the intervening days, and the guide reassesses timing and route carefully before committing to the final approach. Reaching the pass a second time offers one more chance to take in the full sweep of the Bara Bhangal valley behind and the familiar Kangra-side terrain ahead, a view that somehow feels different, more earned, on this return pass. The descent toward Thamsar Base Camp retraces the outbound route, and arriving back at this now-familiar staging camp by evening closes out what is comfortably one of the two longest, most physically demanding days of the entire nine-day trek. Fatigue is considerable at this point, and most groups prioritize an early dinner and immediate rest over any extended evening activity. This double crossing of Thamsar Pass, out and back within the same trek, is a defining feature of this particular itinerary, offering a genuinely complete experience of one of the Dhauladhar-Pir Panjal system's classic high passes from both sides.
highlights: Second crossing of Thamsar Pass, this time returning toward Kangra
Fresh assessment of changed snow conditions from the outbound trip
Familiar Base Camp staging ground reached by evening
One of the two longest, most demanding days of the entire trek
tips: Expect this day to feel harder than the outbound crossing due to accumulated fatigue
Trust the guide's fresh assessment of changed snow conditions
Prioritize rest immediately on arrival; save reflection for tomorrow

thamsar-pass-trek.title: Thamsar Base Camp to Bir Billing
thamsar-pass-trek.trailType: Long descent retracing meadow and forest trail back to Billing
thamsar-pass-trek.accommodation: End of trek; guesthouse in Bir
thamsar-pass-trek.description: The final day covers the entire remaining distance back to Bir Billing in one long push, retracing three days of outbound trekking through Palachak's meadows, Rajgundha's fields, and finally the familiar forest above Billing's paragliding strip. The descent from base camp through the rocky upper valley goes quickly on legs now well broken-in to the terrain, though genuine tiredness from the previous day's double pass crossing makes even straightforward ground feel more effortful than it did on the way up. Palachak's meadow arrives by mid-morning for most groups, offering a natural rest stop and a last look at the wildflower-strewn grass before the trail drops into forest for good. Rajgundha, reached by early afternoon, marks a return to genuine civilization after more than a week spent largely in remote, uninhabited terrain, and many groups pause for a final tea or meal at a familiar homestay before the last stretch down to Billing. The forest descent below the village passes through increasingly familiar territory, streams and log bridges crossed in the opposite direction now, and the first sight of paragliders overhead near Billing signals the practical end of the mountain portion of the trek. Arriving back in Bir by late afternoon completes a genuinely classic nine-day Himalayan crossing, one that combines a serious high pass, one of Himachal's most isolated villages, and a full return journey within a single continuous itinerary. A celebratory meal in Bir, reflecting on both the outbound crossing and the demanding return, provides a fitting close to what many participants describe as among the most complete Dhauladhar treks available.
highlights: Long final descent retracing Palachak and Rajgundha in reverse
Familiar homestay stop in Rajgundha before the last stretch
Return to Bir Billing's paragliding activity signaling the trek's end
Completion of a full nine-day round-trip crossing of Thamsar Pass
tips: Pace yourself; fatigue from the double crossing lingers into this final day
Stop for a final meal in Rajgundha before the last push to Billing
Plan a rest day in Bir after the trek concludes

rajgundha-trek.title: Billing to Rajgundha
rajgundha-trek.trailType: Forest jeep track and footpath through rhododendron
rajgundha-trek.description: Rajgundha has become something of a favorite among trekkers looking for a genuinely satisfying overnight escape without the commitment of a multi-day pass crossing, and the walk in from Billing reflects that easier character from the outset. The jeep track above Billing's paragliding ground is wide and well-graded for the first stretch, gradually narrowing into forest footpath as it climbs through stands of rhododendron that bloom in vivid red and pink through April and May, drawing a steady stream of photographers during peak flowering season. Birdlife is notably active on this route, with sunbirds and various species of laughing thrush frequently spotted flitting through the understory, and the relatively gentle gradient makes this an accessible option even for families or less experienced trekkers. Rajgundha itself sits on a broad shelf of meadow and terraced field, its houses scattered rather than clustered tightly, giving the village an open, relaxed feel distinct from more densely built settlements elsewhere in the region. Arriving by early-to-mid afternoon leaves ample time to settle into camp or a homestay, explore the immediate village lanes, and simply enjoy the mountain air before evening. Views from the village edge extend toward the higher Dhauladhar ridgeline, though the true drama of that landscape is reserved for the following day's exploration higher up. Nights here, well away from any significant light pollution, offer excellent stargazing, and many groups spend a good portion of the evening simply lying back on the grass watching the sky before retiring for a comfortable, unhurried first night.
highlights: Rhododendron blooms along the forest trail in April-May
Active birdlife including sunbirds and laughing thrush
Relaxed, family-friendly overnight trek without a pass to cross
Excellent stargazing from Rajgundha's meadow
tips: Visit in April-May for peak rhododendron flowering
This is a good choice for families or first-time overnight trekkers
Bring a mat for comfortable stargazing on the meadow grass

rajgundha-trek.title: Exploration Day: Rajgundha Upper Meadows
rajgundha-trek.trailType: Meadow trail to a grassy viewpoint shoulder
rajgundha-trek.description: With no onward pass to worry about, the second day is dedicated entirely to exploring the meadow country above Rajgundha, following a gently rising trail toward a broad grassy shoulder that offers considerably wider views than the village itself. The walk up passes through scattered stands of birch and juniper, thinning gradually as the trail gains height, and marmots are a common sight darting between rocks on sunnier mornings, their sharp alarm calls often the first sign of their presence before they're actually spotted. From the high point of this exploration, the view extends across a series of ridgelines toward the deeper Dhauladhar interior, including distant glimpses of higher, snow-streaked peaks that remain completely hidden from the village below. This vantage point also offers a rough sense of the terrain used by longer treks heading toward Thamsar Pass and Bara Bhangal, without any obligation to actually undertake that considerably more demanding route. A leisurely lunch on the grass here, with essentially uninterrupted mountain views in every direction, is one of the trek's genuine highlights, and groups often linger well beyond a typical trail-lunch stop given the lack of any onward schedule pressure. The descent back to Rajgundha is quick and relaxed, following the same trail in reverse, and the remainder of the afternoon is generally left free for rest, village exploration, or simply enjoying a second unhurried evening in the meadow. This day captures much of what makes Rajgundha appealing as a short escape, genuine high-altitude scenery and a real sense of remoteness, achieved without the physical demands or logistics of a proper multi-day expedition.
highlights: Marmot sightings on the meadow slopes above the village
Wide views toward the deeper Dhauladhar interior
Distant glimpse of terrain used by longer Thamsar-bound treks
Leisurely, unhurried lunch stop with panoramic views
tips: Move quietly to spot marmots before they duck into their burrows
Bring a proper lunch to enjoy at the viewpoint without rushing
Use the free afternoon back at Rajgundha for genuine rest

rajgundha-trek.title: Rajgundha to Billing
rajgundha-trek.trailType: Descending forest trail to the paragliding ground
rajgundha-trek.accommodation: End of trek; return to Bir/Billing
rajgundha-trek.description: The return to Billing follows the same forest trail used on the way in, though the descent feels considerably easier on legs that have had a full day to adjust to the altitude and terrain. Rhododendron stands that offered vivid color on the way up look slightly different in the reversed light and angle of the return journey, and the birdlife that was so active on the ascent seems, if anything, even more vocal in the cooler morning hours typically chosen for this final walk down. The jeep track reappears roughly two-thirds of the way down, and from there the walking becomes noticeably easier, wide and even underfoot compared to the narrower forest footpath above. Billing's paragliding ground comes into view well before the descent actually ends, gliders already circling overhead on most mornings with decent wind, a lively contrast to the quiet meadow left behind at Rajgundha. Arriving back at Billing by late morning or early afternoon leaves ample time for onward travel to Bir or elsewhere in the Kangra valley, and many trekkers choose to extend their day with a tandem paragliding flight from the very ground where the trek concludes, combining two of the region's signature outdoor activities into a single satisfying finish. This short, accessible three-day trek offers a genuine taste of Dhauladhar meadow scenery and village life without demanding extensive prior trekking experience, making it a popular choice for those easing into Himalayan trekking or short on time but unwilling to compromise on scenery. Onward buses and taxis to Dharamshala, Bir and Palampur run regularly from Bir's main square, making it easy to continue straight on to the next stop of a wider Kangra itinerary.
highlights: Easier reversed descent through rhododendron forest
Return to Billing's active paragliding launch ground
Option to combine the trek's end with a tandem paragliding flight
Accessible three-day format suited to first-time Himalayan trekkers
tips: Consider a tandem paragliding flight right at the trek's end point
Start the descent in the cooler morning hours for the best birdlife activity
Arrange onward transport from Billing to Bir in advance during peak season

bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.title: Bir to Rajgundha via Billing
bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.trailType: Road, jeep track and forest trail from the valley floor to the meadows
bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.description: Unlike routes that start higher up at Billing, this trek begins right in Bir itself, home to a significant Tibetan refugee settlement whose monasteries and prayer wheels give the town's lower lanes a distinctly different atmosphere from typical Himachal villages. The walk out of town initially follows a paved road past tea gardens that have made this pocket of Kangra locally famous for a distinct, slightly smoky variety of tea, before the route turns onto a dirt track climbing toward Billing. This first stretch gains height quickly, and the temperature difference between Bir's warm valley floor and the cooler forest above becomes noticeable within the first hour or two of walking. Paragliders drift down toward Bir's landing ground at regular intervals throughout the climb, their canopies visible through gaps in the trees, a reminder of the area's other major identity as one of India's premier paragliding destinations. Beyond Billing's grassy launch strip, the trail narrows into proper forest footpath, climbing through rhododendron and oak toward Rajgundha in a stretch that covers considerably more elevation in a single push than trips starting higher up at Billing itself. Arriving at Rajgundha by late afternoon after a genuinely full day of climbing, most groups appreciate the meadow's flat, open ground and cooler air after the heat of the valley below. This condensed two-day format suits weekend travelers or those with limited time who still want a genuine taste of Dhauladhar meadow scenery, achieved through a single demanding but rewarding day of ascent from the valley floor itself.
highlights: Start in Bir's Tibetan settlement with monasteries and prayer wheels
Bir's distinctive local tea gardens on the lower approach
Significant single-day elevation gain from valley floor to meadow
Compact two-day format suited to limited-time weekend travelers
tips: Visit a Bir monastery before or after the trek if time allows
Start early; this route covers more elevation gain in one day than higher-starting alternatives
Carry extra water for the warmer lower stretch out of Bir

bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.title: Rajgundha to Bir
bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.trailType: Long descending forest trail back to the valley floor
bir-billing-to-rajgundha-trek.description: The return to Bir retraces the previous day's considerable elevation loss in a single long downhill push, the meadow air of Rajgundha giving way progressively to warmer forest and eventually the noticeably hotter conditions of the valley floor by the time Bir itself comes back into view. The forest descent passes quickly, legs finding an easy rhythm on ground now familiar from the climb up, though the sheer length of the descent, covering the full elevation difference between meadow and valley in one day, makes this a genuinely tiring final stretch despite requiring no technical skill. Billing's paragliding strip reappears partway down, busier in the afternoon than it typically is during the earlier morning hours, and many groups pause briefly to watch a few flights before continuing the final descent into Bir. The tea gardens near town reappear as the trail rejoins the paved road, and the temperature climbs noticeably on this last stretch, a reminder of just how much cooler Rajgundha's meadow air felt by comparison. Arriving back in Bir by mid-to-late afternoon leaves time to explore the town's Tibetan monasteries properly, something often rushed on the first day given the trek's early departure, or to book a same-day paragliding flight for those wanting to combine both of the area's signature activities within a single compact trip. This efficient two-day round trip delivers genuine high-altitude meadow scenery and a full immersion in Bir's distinct Tibetan-influenced culture, all within a schedule short enough to fit comfortably into even a brief Himachal visit.
highlights: Full single-day descent from meadow back to the warm valley floor
Second look at Billing's paragliding activity on the way down
Time to properly explore Bir's Tibetan monasteries afterward
Efficient two-day round trip fitting a brief Himachal visit
tips: Book a same-day paragliding flight in Bir if you have the energy left
Carry sun protection for the noticeably hotter lower valley stretch
Explore Bir's monasteries properly now that the trek has concluded

barot-valley-trek.title: Barot to the Nargu Forest Camp
barot-valley-trek.trailType: Riverside trail along the Uhl into Nargu forest
barot-valley-trek.description: Barot owes its unusual character to a century-old hydroelectric project, the Shanan Power House, whose network of channels and pipelines still shapes the town's layout and gives it an oddly industrial backdrop against otherwise classic Himachal mountain scenery. The trek begins by following the Uhl river upstream past the trout hatchery that has made Barot a modest but genuine destination for anglers, with clear pools visible from the trail where trout can often be spotted holding steady against the current. Beyond the hatchery, the trail enters the fringes of the Nargu Wildlife Sanctuary, a protected forest known for its population of Himalayan black bear, leopard and a healthy variety of pheasant species, though sightings of the larger mammals remain rare given their understandably cautious nature around human trails. The forest here is dense and genuinely wild-feeling despite the relatively short distance from town, with tall deodar and a thick understory that muffles most sound beyond birdsong and the constant background note of the river. Camp is set on a clearing above the river with views back down the valley toward Barot's scattered rooftops, and evenings here often include the sound of fishing enthusiasts still working the pools well after sunset with a torch. This gentle, low-effort trek suits travelers looking for genuine river and forest scenery without significant altitude or physical demands, and it pairs particularly well with an extended stay in Barot itself, a town that has quietly built a reputation among Himachal insiders as an underrated, uncrowded escape.
highlights: Barot's century-old Shanan Power House and trout hatchery
Edge of the Nargu Wildlife Sanctuary with black bear and leopard habitat
Deodar forest walk along the Uhl river
Low-effort trek ideal for a relaxed river-valley escape
tips: Watch the hatchery pools for trout before starting the walk
Keep noise down near the sanctuary edge for a chance at wildlife sightings
This is a gentle trek; suitable even for those short on trekking fitness

barot-valley-trek.title: Camp to Barot via the Orchard Villages
barot-valley-trek.trailType: Orchard village trail back along the Uhl river
barot-valley-trek.description: The return route takes a slightly different line than the previous day's approach, dropping through a small village whose terraced fields and orchards offer a pleasant contrast to the dense forest camp left behind that morning. Apple trees, increasingly common across this part of Himachal as the local economy shifts toward horticulture, line several stretches of the descent, and depending on season, villagers might be seen harvesting or pruning in the orchards along the way. The trail rejoins the Uhl river a little upstream of where the first day's walk began, and the sound of water grows steadily louder as the path descends back toward the valley floor. A different section of riverbank offers a good final look at the trout pools before the trail crosses back into Barot proper, passing near the Shanan Power House's distinctive old pipeline structure, an unexpectedly photogenic piece of colonial-era engineering set against the mountain backdrop. This short, easy final day leaves plenty of time to explore Barot's own low-key attractions before departing, including the artificial lake created by the hydro project, popular for a leisurely walk or simple boating, and a handful of local cafes serving fresh trout for those wanting to sample the valley's signature catch. Barot rewards a slightly longer stay than most visitors initially plan, and this compact two-day trek serves well as an introduction to a valley that manages to combine industrial history, wildlife habitat and classic river scenery within a genuinely uncrowded and relaxed setting. Onward connections toward Jogindernagar or Mandi are straightforward from Barot's small bus stand, making it easy to fold this short trek into a longer Himachal itinerary.
highlights: Alternate return route through orchard villages
Historic Shanan Power House pipeline structure
Barot's artificial lake for a post-trek stroll
Fresh trout available at local cafes to close out the trip
tips: Sample fresh trout at a Barot cafe after finishing the trek
Take a stroll around Barot's artificial lake if time allows
Consider extending your stay; Barot rewards a slower visit

chhota-bhanghal-trek.title: Barot to Multhan through the Walnut Orchards
chhota-bhanghal-trek.trailType: Village road and forest trail past walnut orchards
chhota-bhanghal-trek.description: Chhota Bhangal, meaning 'Little Bhangal' to distinguish it from its far more famous and remote neighbor Bara Bhangal, is a valley many travelers to Himachal have never heard of despite sitting just a few days' walk from the well-known trails around Bir and Barot. The trek begins in Barot, following a road-turned-trail along the Uhl river before branching off toward the interior villages that give this valley its distinct character, passing through Jhatingri, a small settlement known locally for its walnut orchards that turn the hillside a deep green through summer. The trail alternates between quiet farm lanes and forest footpath, with the villages along the way retaining an unhurried, almost forgotten quality compared to the increasingly trekker-oriented settlements further east near Bir. Multhan, reached by afternoon, serves as the valley's informal center, a crossroads village where paths toward Jalsu Pass and deeper into Chhota Bhangal's interior meadows diverge, though this particular trek's focus stays firmly within the valley itself rather than pushing toward any pass. Village elders here often speak with quiet pride about Chhota Bhangal's relative self-sufficiency, growing much of its own grain and vegetables in fields that have supported the same families for generations. Homestay accommodation offers genuine insight into daily valley life, with evening meals typically featuring simple, hearty local dishes built around whatever the season's harvest provides. This first day sets an unhurried, exploratory tone distinct from the pass-focused treks that also pass through this same village, emphasizing cultural immersion over any particular mountaineering objective.
highlights: Walnut orchards of Jhatingri village
Multhan as the valley's cultural crossroads
Genuine, unhurried homestay culture distinct from busier trek routes
Exploration-focused itinerary within Chhota Bhangal itself
tips: Try a homestay meal built around the season's local harvest
Ask elders about the valley's history of self-sufficiency
Take time in villages; this trek rewards a slower pace

chhota-bhanghal-trek.title: Multhan to Devi Kothi Meadow
chhota-bhanghal-trek.trailType: Mixed forest trail opening into meadow
chhota-bhanghal-trek.description: Beyond Multhan, the trail leaves the last significant cultivation behind and climbs into the mixed forest that covers much of Chhota Bhangal's upper slopes, oak and horse chestnut giving way gradually to fir and spruce as elevation increases. A small shrine dedicated to a local goddess, giving Devi Kothi its name, sits at the edge of the meadow that serves as the day's camp, modest in construction but clearly significant to the shepherd families who pass through here each summer en route to higher pastures. The meadow itself opens onto genuinely wide views for the first time on this trek, with the deeper Dhauladhar interior visible in layered ridgelines extending north, distinctly different from anything seen in the villages below. Streams cut across the grass at a couple of points, clear and cold, providing reliable water for camp, and the relative flatness of the meadow makes for comfortable tent pitching compared to some of the steeper camps found on more technical treks in the region. Wildflowers are abundant here through much of the summer, and the meadow's isolation means genuine quiet, broken mostly by wind and the occasional call of a Himalayan bird rather than any human activity. This camp offers a good sense of the valley's scale without requiring the technical commitment of a full pass crossing, and evenings here are typically spent simply enjoying the view and the quiet, a fitting continuation of the trek's exploratory, unhurried character established the day before. Firewood is scarce this high, so most groups rely on a gas stove for cooking rather than the open fires common lower down in Multhan.
highlights: Devi Kothi's small goddess shrine at the meadow's edge
First wide views into the deeper Dhauladhar interior
Comfortable, flat meadow camping with reliable stream water
Genuine quiet and isolation without technical pass-crossing demands
tips: Respect the local shrine; it holds real significance for shepherd families
Pitch tents on the flatter sections for the most comfortable night
Enjoy the quiet; this meadow sees far fewer visitors than busier trek camps

chhota-bhanghal-trek.title: Exploration Day: Ridge Above Devi Kothi
chhota-bhanghal-trek.description: With the valley's interior meadows as a base, the third day is dedicated to a day hike up a rocky ridge above Devi Kothi that delivers arguably the trek's best views without demanding any particular technical skill or extended distance. The climb starts across open meadow before transitioning to looser rock and scree in the final stretch, gaining enough height to reveal, for the first time, a genuine sense of how Chhota Bhangal connects to the wider network of valleys and passes that define this pocket of the Dhauladhar, including distant glimpses toward terrain used by the more demanding treks toward Jalsu Pass and Bara Bhangal. Marmots and occasional Himalayan tahr have been spotted on this ridge by past groups, though sightings depend heavily on the time of day and how quietly the group moves. From the top, a genuinely panoramic view unfolds, ridgelines folding into each other in every direction, with Devi Kothi's meadow reduced to a small green patch far below and the villages of the lower valley barely visible in the haze toward the south. A relaxed lunch at this vantage point, with no particular schedule pressure, allows time to properly absorb a landscape that very few outside visitors ever see given how little-known this particular valley remains compared to its neighbors. The descent back to camp is straightforward, and the remaining afternoon is left free for rest or a final wander around the meadow, a fitting close to the trek's main exploratory objective before the return journey begins the following day.
highlights: Panoramic ridge viewpoint above Devi Kothi meadow
Possible Himalayan tahr and marmot sightings
Distant orientation toward Jalsu Pass and Bara Bhangal terrain
Relaxed, schedule-free lunch with sweeping valley views
tips: Move quietly for the best chance of spotting tahr or marmots
Bring a proper lunch to enjoy unhurried at the viewpoint
Use the free afternoon back at camp for genuine rest

chhota-bhanghal-trek.title: Devi Kothi to Barot
chhota-bhanghal-trek.trailType: Long descent through forest and orchard villages
chhota-bhanghal-trek.description: The return journey retraces the valley's villages and forest in a single long day, dropping from Devi Kothi's meadow back through fir and oak forest before rejoining the farmland surrounding Multhan by early afternoon. The walnut orchards of Jhatingri, passed again on this final stretch, look different in the reversed light of a descending afternoon, and a brief stop here for tea is a common way to break up the day's remaining distance.
